experiments.title: How gift array options in a high urgency sticky bar impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the HUC sticky bar for IMB website visitors will increase engagement and donor conversion by minimizing the number of steps on the donation page, reducing friction and decision-making, resulting in more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2026 - 01/19/2026
During IMB’s 2026 Winter Olympics campaign, we re-tested the sticky bar’s gift array buttons to understand their impact on donor behavior.
In reviewing the original execution, we identified opportunities to improve how the sticky bar guided visitors toward giving—particularly around campaign value proposition and giving array amounts. We re-tested this approach to determine whether modifying the sticky bar could more effectively encourage gifts and to gather data needed to inform future fundraising tactics.
For this test, 50% of site traffic was shown the standard sticky bar experience featuring a single call-to-action button. The remaining 50% received a treatment version with three buttons mirroring the suggested gift amounts on the donation page, allowing visitors to engage with a giving option earlier in their journey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment shows that adding a gift array to the IMB website’s sticky bar significantly increased both visitor engagement and donor conversion. The treatment version—featuring three suggested gift amount buttons—outperformed the standard sticky bar by 448.4% in Clicks for All Traffic, with 99.9% statistical confidence.
These results suggest that introducing giving options earlier in the donor journey reduces friction and simplifies decision-making on the donation page—leading to more completed gifts.
Downstream Impact on Donations:
To understand how increased engagement translated into giving behavior, we analyzed downstream donation metrics. During the test period, the control version generated 1 gift, while the treatment generated 16 gifts, representing a 1,495% increase in transactions (100% LOC).
This confirms that the gift array not only drives more clicks but also meaningfully motivates visitors to complete a donation.
Gift Amount Performance:
We also evaluated which gift array buttons led to the most donations:
$25 Button: 6 gifts, all at $25
 	$50 Button: 3 gifts, all at $50
 	$100 Button: 3 gifts (2 at $100, 1 at $150)
 	More Options: 4 gifts, resulting in varied amounts ($20, $50, $50, $200)
Notably, most donors retained the amount they selected in the sticky bar through to the completion of the donation, reinforcing the value of early commitment.
Conclusion & Recommendation:
Overall, the gift array on the sticky bar effectively removes a key decision point from the donation page, leading to higher engagement and more completed gifts.
For future testing, IMB should continue exploring gift arrays and similar friction-reducing elements in high-visibility areas of the site. Ongoing iteration will help further optimize the giving experience and maximize donor conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: How a donor goal on a high urgency campaign pushdown impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors will increase donor conversion metrics because potential donors will feel their monthly gift would have a larger impact on the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/03/2025
During The Navigators’ February Sustainers Campaign, we decided to test adding a donor goal into the pushdown copy.
The first treatment variation set a blanket donor goal of 100 monthly donors, and the second treatment variation set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array (i.e. Will you be one of the 50 monthly donors to make a $15 monthly gift, 30 donors to make a $25 monthly gift, or 20 donors to make a $50 monthly gift today?)
The goal of this test was to determine whether The Navigators’ audience would respond positively to a defined donor goal and, if so, whether they were more motivated by an overarching goal or by a goal broken into smaller amounts that felt more attainable.
Our hypothesis was that breaking down the donor goal into smaller, specific amounts would make it feel more achievable, increasing the likelihood of giving. By seeing a clearer, tangible impact, donors would be more inclined to contribute, believing their gift could make a greater difference.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors significantly increased donor conversion metrics. The treatment variation that set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array resulted in a 266.8% increase in donations across all traffic compared to the control group, with a 96.8% statistical level of confidence. It is possible that Treatment #2 caused more confusion and friction on the page, rather than the very clear goal of 100 donors in Treatment #1. This suggests that The Navigators' audience reacts positively to clarity and set goal amounts. Furthermore, Treatment #2 had a 301% lift in revenue at a 97.4% confidence level. Not only did the overall donor goal motivate more people to give, but it also motivated them to give more than in the control group. In future experimentation, we should continue to incorporate donor goals into our fundraising campaigns. By setting specific, attainable donor goals, donors are more likely to feel their contributions will make a difference and be more motivated to give. Additionally, breaking the overall donor goal into smaller amounts can make it seem more achievable and encourage more donors to participate. Overall, the success of this experiment demonstrates the importance of setting donor goals and providing donors with a clear understanding of how their contributions can make an impact. This strategy can be utilized in future fundraising efforts to drive donor engagement and increase donations for The Navigators.

experiments.title: How Adding a One-Time Gift Option to a Slide-Up Promoting Recurring Giving Impacted Transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a one-time gift option to the slide-up will lead to more transactions overall, while maintaining a similar number of sustaining gifts. This approach is designed to attract individuals who may not be ready to commit to a sustaining gift, while still capturing those who were already inclined to make a sustaining contribution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for a public radio station serving North Carolina, during their February Fund Drive. We aimed to test two different slide-ups: one was a previous version that encouraged prospects to make a sustaining gift, while in the test version, we added an option for a one-time gift to see if it would result in more overall gifts, including sustaining contributions.
Everything else remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In conclusion, the results of this experiment indicate that the control version, which emphasized monthly sustaining donations, outperformed the treatment version, which included both one-time and sustaining gift options. The control version generated 53 gifts and $1,510 in revenue, compared to the treatment version's 23 gifts and $595 in revenue.
Breaking down the results further:
The control version had 15 one-time gifts and 38 recurring gifts.
 	The treatment version had 6 one-time gifts and 17 recurring gifts.
 	The control version also saw more first-time gifts, with 35 first-time gifts compared to the treatment's 13.
A key finding was that the control version resulted in a 58% higher donation volume across all traffic than the treatment version, with 100% statistical confidence. This suggests that focusing solely on sustaining donations was more effective at driving contributions, as adding a one-time gift option did not increase the total number of donations.
Additionally, four sustainers made another one-time donation, all through the control version.
Moving forward, it would be beneficial to continue emphasizing the importance and impact of monthly sustaining gifts, as this approach appears to resonate more effectively with prospects. Further testing could explore different messaging strategies and donation options to identify the most effective combination for future fundraising campaigns. These insights will be key to optimizing the approach to maximizing donations in the future.

experiments.title: How decreasing the recommended gift amount also decreased conversion rate on a relationship content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the recommended gift amount from $50 to $30 for visitors of the “Healing Parent and Adult Child Relationships”  content offer donation page will achieve an increased donation conversion rate because a lower default ask reduces psychological pressure, making it easier for more people to say yes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2026 - 01/20/2026
Focus on the Family is running a video series content offer called “Healing Parent and Adult Child Relationships.” This donation page includes a premium incentive: donors who give $30 or more will receive a copy of the book featured in the video series, and written by the guest speaker.
At the time of this test, the donation page featured a $50 “popular” gift amount, even though the premium threshold was $30. This created a potential mismatch between what donors were being told, and what the page implicitly suggested was the most appropriate gift.
We identified this as a possible source of friction before considering a GOAA (gift of any amount) test for this same page, so we wanted to address this misalignment first. If donors were to notice this misalignment when intending to receive the premium, they might question whether $30 was truly sufficient, or feel uncertain about which amount was expected.
This ran as a split-url test on the donation page, with a 50 – 50 traffic split. The control noted $50 as a popular gift amount, while the treatment noted $30 as popular. No copy, imagery, or premium messaging was changed. Most visitors arrived to this page from meta advertising with strong messaging about the importance of reconnecting with your child before its too late.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Aligning the suggested gift amount with the $30 premium threshold significantly decreased donation conversion rate, resulting in 66% fewer donors compared to the control. This outcome was statistically significant at 99.9% confidence, indicating a clear and meaningful negative impact.
Though the gift array appeared misaligned to the premium ask, the data suggests that it functioned as a strong value and intent signal.
Even with a $30 premium clearly stated elsewhere on the page, the higher default likely:
Anchored donors to a more serious level of commitmentReinforced the perceived value of the contentReduced ambiguity about what constituted a meaningful gift
By shifting the default to $30, the page may have unintentionally reframed the experience as more transactional (giving to get the book) rather than mission driven (giving to support their work), weakening donor motivation.
This result highlights that clarity is not always the primary driver of motivation. In some contexts, the donor may be able to ignore small inconsistencies if the overall framing of the donation ask signals strong importance and impact.
For future testing, we should keep in mind the type of content we are testing on. For emotionally resonant or mission-driven content, a higher default may increase confidence and commitment, rather than deter giving. If audiences feel compelled enough by the messaging of the offer, lowering the barrier may not be the best approach.

defaulting the tabbed form to monthly for paid media users on instant donation pages will achieve an increase in recurring giving, while not negatively impacting overall transactions because it treats monthly giving like a priority, suggesting it as the primary way of giving, versus an after-thought or add-on.

Time of Grace had recently tested into and rolled out a valid increase in overall transactions, when using a tabbed form for paid media traffic. We hypothesize that defaulting the tab to monthly would passively increase recurring giving while not having a negative impact on overall transactions.

Overall Transactions:
Control: 3,452 Samples | 151 Donations |
Treatment: 3,652 Samples | 172 Donations | +10% Increase with 64% LOC.
Finally, within the time frame of this experiment being run only one donor reached out to cancel their monthly gift. The impact of this experiment is that Time of Grace can now begin passively acquiring recurring donors from their paid media traffic, without any increase to budget or a separate monthly campaign.

experiments.title: Can a lower, non-preselected gift array improve conversion rate and revenue across acquisition-focused content offers?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using a lower gift array without preselecting an amount for acquisition audiences would achieve higher conversion rates and competitive revenue because reducing perceived commitment and decision friction would encourage first-time donors to complete a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/22/2025
The Hoover Institution continues to grow its fundraising program by standardizing high-impact donation form elements while preserving flexibility by offer and channel. As part of this effort, Hoover evaluated gift array size and preselection as scalable levers that influence both donor conversion and revenue performance across predominantly acquisition-focused content offers.
This write-up summarizes aggregate performance across multiple offers, focusing on conversion rate, revenue, and average order value (AOV). Four variants were tested: a higher gift array ($50/100/250) and a lower gift array ($25/50/100), each with and without preselecting the second gift amount. The goal was to identify a default configuration that maximizes acquisition efficiency without materially compromising gift value.
experiments.keyLearnings:
At the aggregate level, the experiment produced a statistically valid conversion rate winner, with directional but informative revenue and AOV patterns.
📊 Conversion Rate (Statistically Evaluated)
By individual variant
Low array, no selectConversion rate: 6.2%Lift vs. high array, no select: +32.1%Confidence: 96.0% (statistically valid) ✅High array, no select (control): 4.7%Low array, select: 5.6% (not statistically valid)High array, select: 4.3%
By aggregated comparison
Low array vs. high array (all traffic combined)Low array CR: 5.93%High array CR: 4.52%Confidence: ~99.5% (statistically valid) ✅No select vs. selectNo select CR: 5.48%Select CR: 4.96%Confidence: ~70% (not valid) ❌
Takeaway:
The lower gift array is a statistically proven driver of higher conversion rate. Preselection does not provide a reliable conversion benefit at the aggregate level.
💰 Revenue (Directional)
Revenue results did not reach statistical validity and should be interpreted directionally.
High array, no select: $3,951Low array, no select: $4,249 (highest-performing no-select variant)High array, select: $4,309Low array, select: $3,634
While the high array with preselection generated the most total revenue, it did so with lower conversion efficiency and without statistical confidence. The low array without preselection delivered strong total revenue while also maximizing donor acquisition, making it the most balanced option for acquisition-focused goals.
🎯 Average Order Value (AOV – Directional)
High array (combined): ~$46.40Low array (combined): ~$34.73Select (combined): ~$40.73No select (combined): ~$39.05
Preselection and higher arrays increase AOV, but these gains consistently come with conversion tradeoffs, particularly for acquisition traffic.
✂️ Aggregate Interpretation
The lower gift array without preselection is the only variant that is statistically validated to improve conversion rateRevenue differences are directional, but the low array/no select performs competitively while acquiring more donorsPreselecting any amount introduces friction and does not produce consistent gains in conversion or revenue at scaleHigher arrays may be situationally valuable, but should be deployed intentionally and tested by offer
Next Steps
Roll out the lower gift array without preselection as the default for predominantly acquisition-focused content offersDo not preselect a gift amount in the standard configurationUse this result as a new baseline, not a permanent winnerTest deviations by offer and channel, requiring ≥90% statistical significance to override the defaultPlan future tests during offer launches and explore donation page copy and design as higher-impact leversRe-test as the program, traffic mix, and donation forms evolve
Rather than treating A/B testing as a one-time selection of a winner, this approach supports a living optimization framework, where standards are periodically challenged as strategies and audiences change.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2025 - 12/08/2025

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The slideout with a gift array on the homepage of CaringBridge resulted in a significant increase in donations for all traffic, with a 73.6% increase at a 98.7% level of confidence.
2. In particular, the One-Time Donation option saw a remarkable 672% increase with a 98% level of confidence.
3. The hypothesis that the slideout with a gift array would reduce friction for users while making a gift proved to be true.
Moving forward, we should consider implementing the slideout with a gift array on other key pages of the CaringBridge website to optimize donation opportunities. Additionally, we could explore further testing variations of gift arrays to determine which types are most effective in driving donations. It is crucial to continue monitoring and analyzing user behavior to enhance the overall donation experience on the platform.

experiments.title: How a dynamic impact callout beneath the gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A dynamic impact array for users on premium donation pages will increase average gift and donor conversion. It encourages greater interaction with the array and helps readers see their gift’s tangible impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 11/18/2024
One of the best ways to make emails and donation pages feel more personal is by showing donors the real difference their gift can make. In this test, the team added a dynamic callout line beneath the gift array. As supporters clicked through different gift amounts, the message would update in real time to show the impact at that level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a dynamic impact array beneath the gift options led to a significant increase in Transactions, with a 162.3% increase for All Traffic. This indicates that the dynamic callout line effectively encouraged greater interaction with the array and helped donors see the tangible impact of their contributions.

2. Revenue also experienced a substantial increase of 168% with a 98% confidence level.

This suggests that the dynamic impact array not only increased the number of transactions but also positively impacted the average gift amount.
Although on closer inspection, it's clear that the spike in average gift is due to a larger outlier gift, it is still worth noting that no gift surpassed $50 in the control. In contrast, the outlier gift was coupled with multiple gifts between 25 and 50, rather than the typical $10 average gift.
Moving forward, we should continue to consider how the gift array itself can be leveraged alongside this impact statement to achieve the greatest improvement in the average gift.

experiments.title: How a slide up with a gift array performs against a pop-up with a simple donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering people specific gift asks for the fund drive will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2025 - 08/13/2025
During a special fund drive for OPB, the public media broadcasting station for Oregon and Southwest Washington, we wanted to test how a standard pop-up with a simple donate button (what OPB traditionally uses during a fund drive) would perform against a slide-up that included specific gift arrays.
This test was run on article pages only; a separate test was run on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There were some exciting results from this experiment. The unique fundraising environment for this test is not something that cannot be easily repeatable, unless there is another urgent/emergency campaign. That being said, the increase in conversion rate from the slide up gift array version is huge and statistically valid. The results above can be directly attributed to people clicking the button/array and completing their gift.

A couple of interesting downstream results to be aware of:
The control version had a slightly higher average gift than the treatment version; given the lower volume of gifts on the control, that could be the influence of a larger gift or two, or the fact that we were showing donors a gift array up front in the treatment version, leading them to give a lower average gift (gift arrays on the actual donation forms were the same for both control and treatment).
 	Regarding the overall influence both had on total giving, the gift array version had a slightly larger influence, showing a 23% increase in conversion rates overall with a 96% level of confidence. This means that people who saw the gift array version and then made a gift at some point to a donation form on the website (not necessarily the one linked directly from the pop-up) were 23% more likely to convert.

experiments.title: How a slideup with a gift array selection vs a stickybar affects monthly donations for a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a slideup with gift array for website visitors will achieve an increase in donations because that slideup with the gift array would lead them directly to the donation page with their gift pre-filled, hence reducing a good amount of friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2025 - 02/28/2025
Time of Grace had their recurring campaign in the last two weeks of February. We wanted to test how a slideup with a gift array selection vs a stickybar would affect the donor conversion rate. The language on the sticky bar and the slideup was the same, so the elements of the sticky bar and slideup were being tested against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

1. The addition of a slideup with a gift array selection significantly increased the Recurring Donation for All Traffic by 122.7% with a high level of confidence (96.0%). This indicates that the slideup with the gift array was more effective in encouraging donations compared to the sticky bar.

Recommendations for Future Experimentation:

1. Consider the trade-off between increased donations and user engagement when implementing slideups with gift arrays. It may be beneficial to test different variations to find the optimal balance between the two.

2. Use the learnings from this experiment to inform future strategies for maximizing donations while maintaining user engagement. This could involve testing different placements, designs, or messaging to find the most effective approach.

3. Consider conducting follow-up experiments to further refine the approach and iterate on the findings from this experiment. This will help in continuously optimizing the donation process and improving overall performance.

Overall, the key takeaway is to balance the trade-off between increasing donations and maintaining user engagement when implementing similar strategies in the future. The success of the slideup with gift array highlights the importance of reducing friction in the donation process, but it should be done in a way that complements overall website engagement.

experiments.title: The impact of a monthly gift array pushdown on recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a recurring gift pushdown for visitors to the homepage of Heritage.org will achieve a lift in monthly gifts to The Heritage Foundation because of the prioritization of recurring giving and the supporting copy emphasizing the need for sustained support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2025 - 06/01/2025
The Heritage membership team tested a monthly gift pushdown on the homepage of heritage.org. The test was visible to all desktop users on every visit and was run during the last 3 days of the May Leaders Club campaign. The pushdown emphasized the need for fearless patriots to help sustain the mission of the Heritage Foundation before time ran out. It featured 3 clickable buttons with suggested ask amounts and 1 button with “other”. After clicking the user was redirected to a landing page with only a recurring  gift option. In conjunction with the test, a typical campaign banner add was featured below also asking the user to activate their Leaders Club membership.
The test ran as a 20/80 split because of time restraints, with the control receiving 20% of the traffic and the treatment receiving 80%. Overall there was no difference in the number of transactions, but the treatment received 7 monthly gifts while the control received none. Although if the test had been allowed to run longer and the control had received a monthly gift, than these results would not be valid.
Further testing, for a longer period of time is suggested.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Adding a recurring gift pushdown to the homepage of Heritage.org resulted in a 0.1% increase in monthly gifts to The Heritage Foundation.
2. The treatment version, which emphasized the need for sustained support through recurring giving, outperformed the control version.
3. The treatment version had a statistically significant increase in monthly gifts with a 99.2% level of confidence.
4. The treatment received 7 monthly gifts while the control received none, indicating that the recurring gift pushdown was effective in prompting donors to choose monthly giving options.
In future experimentation, we should:
1. Continue to prioritize recurring giving options on the homepage or key landing pages to encourage sustained support.
2. Utilize language that emphasizes the impact of recurring gifts and the urgent need for support in order to motivate donors to take action.
3. Consider running tests for longer periods to gather more data and ensure the validity of results, especially when looking to compare against a control group that may generate unexpected outcomes given more time.
4. Explore additional elements such as different suggested ask amounts or variations in the messaging to further optimize the performance of recurring gift pushdowns.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the effectiveness of emphasizing recurring giving options and the importance of crafting compelling copy that resonates with donors.

experiments.summary:

During Today in the Word’s FYE Campaign, we tested adding gift array buttons into the lower third vs a one-button ask in a lower third. This test ran for 8 days and the treatment was shown to 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:

The treatment resulted in a 172.0% increase in Transactions (src=tw_fye25_footer*) for All Traffic with a 98.2% level of confidence, indicating that the treatment was successful in persuading users to navigate to the donation page and complete a transaction. Based on these results, we should be using a lower third with a gift array in it for future lower thirds for Today in the Word.
We also tracked clicks on both the control and treatment lower thirds and the treatment saw a 51.4% lift in clicks ( 97.1% LOC) compared to the control. These results indicate that the treatment not only encouraged more people to navigate to the donation page, but also resulted in an increase in transcations.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of providing users with the opportunity to select a gift amount before reaching the donation page. This can streamline the giving process for users and potentially lead to higher conversion rates. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing different variations of donation prompts and CTAs to optimize donor conversion metrics further.

experiments.title: How a Testimonial Carousel Compares to a Slide-Up with Gift Array
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a testimony carousel for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because in comparison to the normal slideup, it helps incorporate and visualize the real impact a donor’s gift has on those who use the CaringBridge platform every day.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2024 - 12/14/2024
During CaringBridge’s high-urgency appeal, we hypothesized that a testimony carousel would prove more emotive and effective at converting donors than the standard slide-up. In the control, users were presented with a slide-up that contained three giving options. In the treatment, users were presented with a similar slide-up onsite, but besides the copy was a rotating carousel of real images and stories from the CaringBridge blog about who is impacted by the platform.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the testimony carousel, which was the treatment, did not achieve an increase in donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, it led to a significant decrease of 62.3% in Donations compared to the control.
This outcome suggests that the rotating carousel of real images and stories from the CaringBridge blog may have been less effective or possibly distracting for users, leading to a lower conversion rate. This may also indicate that the effectiveness of the control, is in using that right-hand side of the slideup in order to simplify the giving process rather than expanding on more reasons to give. Having a gift array that prefills on the donation page, is ultimately more impactful on the donor's completion of their gift than the additional testimonies on the carousel.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of conducting thorough testing and being open to unexpected outcomes in order to make informed decisions for future strategies and optimizations.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2024 - 08/23/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a survey on Voter Fraud where people can take the survey and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts to fight fraud in elections. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The treatment’s gift array was $10, $25, and $50.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on Voter Fraud survey donation pages include the following:
1. Decreasing gift array amounts can increase donor conversion rate: The treatment, which presented lower gift amounts of $10, $25, and $50, saw an 18.0% increase in donations, with a valid 98% level of confidence, compared to the control with the $25, $50, and $100 amounts. This indicates that lowering the suggested gift amounts made donating feel more attainable to a larger number of people, leading to a higher conversion rate.
2. Increase in one-time transactions: The treatment group also showed a 16.8% increase in one-time donations at a valid 97% level of confidence, demonstrating that the lower gift array amounts not only attracted more donors, but also encouraged more one-time contributions.
3. Decrease in average gift amount: The treatment group also led to a 14.8% decrease in average gift, indicating that lower gift amounts may lead to people giving lower amounts, but at a 4% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.
Additional testing may include lower gift amounts on other survey pages as well as other types of offers or direct appeals.

experiments.title: How a slide-up with gift array vs sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page during a high-urgency direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a slide-up with a gift array for users on Luther’s Working Preacher site will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2024 - 10/17/2024
Luther Seminary was running a high-urgency campaign to support their Working Preacher site, and one of the web elements on the site was a standard direct appeal sticky bar. We wanted to test whether using a slide-up element instead of a sticky bar would drive more user engagement through clicks to the donation page.
The sticky bar and slide-up both used the same language, but the slide-up presented users with 3 gift options. Clicking a gift option would direct users to the donation page and the form would be pre-filled with their selected gift amount.
Our hypothesis was that the slide-up would drive an increase in clicks to the donation page because it lets users begin the process of giving before they even land on the donation page. We also hoped that this would result in a downstream lift in donations from the slide-up.
We split the website traffic 50/50 between the two variants, and the test ran for ~3 days before we ran out of testing credits and had to end it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a slide-up with a gift array on Luther Seminary's Working Preacher site led to a significant increase in clicks to the donation page compared to a standard sticky bar. The treatment resulted in a 43.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence (99.5%)!
We also found that the slide-up led to a 33.4% increase in donor conversion, but this was only with a 68.3% level of confidence.
From this test we learned that it is important to continue exploring different ways to engage Working Preacher users and streamline the donation process for them. It is clear that providing users with the option to select a gift amount before landing on the donation page can be effective in driving more clicks and potentially increasing donations. For future testing, we want to experiment with the language in the slide-up, as well as the psychology of price anchoring with our gift array amounts.

experiments.title: How a lower gift array impacted conversion on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: The hypothesis is: can we get more people to say yes to making a donation, if we offer a lower value option in the gift array?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2023 - 12/31/2023
We noticed on a previous content offer donation page many people were entering lower amounts than the $50, $100, and $150 options on the page. While the overall average gift for all conversions is right between $50-$60 which was on track with the previous year’s results, we were seeing a range of manual amounts from visitors of $10, $25, $30 and $45 in the “other” field.
With this in mind, we wondered if it might make sense to develop a treatment adding in a fourth gift array option of $25 for the Christmas devotional instant donation page while still keeping the $50 secondary amount as the pre-selected choice and measure conversion response from visitors.
If we could increase conversion by lowering the entry level amount, this could help us easily course-correct the initial revenue decrease we were seeing so far in the year-over-year comparison and get back on track. We launched the test, splitting traffic 50/50 and ran it for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a lower value option in the gift array can significantly increase the number of people willing to make a donation. The treatment version, which included a $25 option along with the $50 and $100 options, saw a 26.2% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control version at a 95.5% level of confidence so the experiment results are valid.
This suggests that some potential donors may be hesitant to give higher amounts and offering a lower option can help overcome this hesitation. By providing more choices and catering to different financial capabilities, nonprofits like Dallas Theological Seminary can encourage more people to contribute.
In future experimentation, it would be valuable to further explore the impact of different gift array options. This could involve testing additional variations, such as offering even lower values or different ranges. Additionally, it would be beneficial to analyze the average gift amounts for each option to understand if the lower value options are affecting overall donation amounts. By continuously iterating and testing different gift array options, nonprofits can optimize their donation pages to maximize conversions and revenue.

experiments.title: How a lowered and reversed gift array impacts monthly donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the gift array with a monthly ask for donors will achieve an increase in recurring gifts because this array is inclusive of more donors as well as realistic to a monthly commitment..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/05/2023 - 10/13/2023
For Reason to Believe’s Monthly Partner campaign, we hypothesized that adjusting the gift array to better align with their monthly ask would increase recurring gifts. In the control, users are primed on the importance of a monthly gift but are met with a gift array that starts $500 and only then decreases to $50 as the lowest monthly option. Our question was whether this could exclude donors who were ready to make a monthly gift but perhaps couldn’t commit to such a large amount and would then abandon the process entirely. By lowering the gift array and reversing it, the starting amount was $10 a month. We believed this better aligned with the monthly commitment and as a result, would increase monthly donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning the gift array with a monthly ask can significantly increase recurring donations. The treatment version, which started the gift array at $10 a month instead of $500, saw a 10.9% increase in recurring donations compared to the control version. This suggests that the previous gift array may have excluded potential donors who were ready to make a monthly commitment but couldn't commit to such a large amount. It should be noted that the treatment not only garnered more gifts, but it received all recurring gifts in the experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing and refining the gift array to further optimize recurring donations. This could involve testing different price points and messaging to find the most effective combination. Additionally, it would be valuable to segment the data and analyze the impact of the treatment on different donor groups, such as new donors or existing monthly donors. This information can help tailor the gift array and messaging for different donor segments to maximize the likelihood of recurring donations.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an impact callout on the $50 amount for potential donors on acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in average gift and revenue because donors will be more likely to convert at higher amount if the impact of that gift is outlined.
experiments.summary:

Reasons to Believe’s paid acquisition efforts are primarily based around free resources. These offers often have no problem gaining traction at the ad level, and they maintain health conversion rates on the landing page and donation page as well. However, a low average gifts has consistently dragged down ROI despite those other data points. After running experiments on the gift array like a “Most Popular” callout to middling results, we identified a different approach for this test. Past wins on RTB’s donation page have often focused the copy around impacting others — we know this audience likes the resources — and are motivated by the prospect of RTB getting them to more people.
Based on this, we hypothesized that highlighting the $50 amount again, but this time with an Impact Callout rather than a “Most Popular” callout would increase average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the impact of a donation can significantly increase average gift size and revenue. By focusing on how the donor's gift can make a difference, we were able to see a 174.5% increase in transactions for all traffic with a 96.1% statistical level of confidence.
Although the original metric targeted for this test, average gift, was relatively similar between control and treatment. The underlying goal of driving revenue and improving ROI was still achieved by the sheer increase in donor conversion. What this indicates is that even if a user could not commit to $50, they were more likely to give still having the impact of a donation to RTB clearly outlined.
In future experimentation, we should continue to emphasize the impact of donations in our messaging. By clearly outlining how a donor's contribution can benefit others, we can motivate donors to give at higher levels. This approach not only helps increase average gift size but also fosters a stronger connection between donors and the cause, potentially leading to higher donor retention rates.

experiments.title: How eliminating the highest gift array option from a donation form impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of gift array options for a non donor audience will achieve an increase in conversion because there is reduced friction in the decision making process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 02/23/2024
When sending non-donor traffic to a donation page after signing a petition, we wondered if having a $200 option in the gift array was necessary or if would harm conversion by making non-donors have more options to consider, and feeling that if they could not make a large gift amount, then they should not give. We wondered if by reducing the friction in the process by taking out the highest option—which we surmised would be the lowest likely option for a non-donor to choose as their first gift—we would see an increase in conversion from this audience. We split traffic 50/50 via email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array options for a non-donor audience led to a significant increase in conversions. By removing the highest option of $200 from the donation page, there was reduced friction in the decision-making process for potential donors, resulting in a 28.5% increase in transactions with 98% level of confidence for the non-donor traffic that qualified for entry in the test. This increase in conversions demonstrates that providing fewer options can help streamline the donation process and encourage more individuals to make a contribution.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of reducing the number of gift array options on conversion rates. This could involve further refining the donation page by eliminating additional high-end donation amounts or simplifying the overall layout to make it easier for potential donors to choose a gift amount. Additionally, it would be valuable to explore how this approach may impact donor retention and overall fundraising success in the long term. By incorporating the learnings from this experiment into future testing and optimization strategies, the Canadian Taxpayers Federation can continue to improve its donation process and drive greater support for its cause.

experiments.title: How suggesting a gift amount on an instant donation page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a $25 donation in the copy letter from Dr. Ben Carson and by pre-selecting it on the gift array will result in an increase in average gift and revenue overall because we’re removing the decision friction for the donor of what amount to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2023 - 12/18/2023
ACI was running a petition to stand up against cancel culture. On the instant donation page following the petition page, users were asked to make a donation in support of ACI and their fight to find solutions against cancel culture. The control page had $25 pre-selected in the gift array, but there was no mention of this ask in the copy from Dr. Ben Carson.
Because the average gift on this page is $19, we thought asking the user to make a $25 gift in the copy would remove decision friction and increase overall average gift and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that asking for a specific donation amount in the copy letter and pre-selecting it on the gift array led to a 68% decrease in conversion rate with a 99% level of confidence. There was no difference in revenue or average gift.
The control version, on the other hand, did not mention a specific amount in the copy and had a $25 pre-selected option in the gift array. This version performed better in terms of donation generation.
Based on these results, it is clear that the specific ask of a $25 donation created decision friction for the donors. By removing this ask from the copy and pre-selecting a default option in the gift array, the control version was able to provide a more frictionless giving experience and generate higher revenue.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of specific donation asks and pre-selected options on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to test different donation amounts, language, and default options to optimize for higher revenue and average gift. Additionally, it would be prudent to carefully analyze the potential consequences of any changes in copy or design to ensure that they align with the objectives of the nonprofit and do not negatively impact donation performance.

experiments.title: How calling out a most popular gift option on a gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the inclusion of a “most popular” callout on a gift array  for the $50 option for potential donors will achieve a higher average gift because it leverages the psychological principle of social proof, creating a sense of trust and legitimacy among potential donors.

The American Cornerstone Institute is offering a book called “Why America Matters” for a gift of $25 or more through paid Facebook ads. We wanted to test adding a most popular callout to the $50 option on the gift array to see if donors would give a larger amount to receive the book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a "most popular" callout on a gift array for a specific option can significantly increase donations and average gift. The treatment version, which included a "most popular" callout on the $50 option, resulted in a 27.5% increase in donations compared to the control version. Additionally, the treatment saw a $0.35 increase in gift size with a 95.46% level of confidence.
This experiment demonstrates the power of social proof, where potential donors are influenced by the choices made by others. By highlighting a specific option as the "most popular," potential donors are more likely to trust and perceive it as a legitimate choice, leading to a higher average gift.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the use of social proof in different ways. For example, we can test different callouts such as "best value" or "preferred choice" to see if they have a similar impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore the effect of social proof in other aspects of the donation process, such as the donation page layout or testimonials from previous donors.

Additionally, analyzing the data from this experiment can provide insights into the specific donor segments that responded most positively to the "Give Monthly" button, allowing for targeted marketing efforts in the future.

experiments.title: How reducing decision friction in the gift array impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing decision friction in the gift array for prospective donors coming from paid traffic will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the cognitive momentum will be improved with fewer choices and more social proof as guidance when choosing an amount from the array.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2023 - 09/29/2023
When converting a housefile offer to acquisition traffic, we reviewed the donation page and wondered if the gift array for the housefile was too complex for traffic that did not have as close of an affinity for the organization or have a previous history of giving. We wanted to test tailoring the gift array to the type of visitors we would be sending to the page and we wondered if a 6 option gift array with the majority of options over $100 would cause friction in the process for these prospective donors. We split traffic between the existing control gift array ranging from $25 to $25,000 and lowered the barrier for entry by making all gift options amounts under $100 as well as giving guidance/social proof pointing to the most popular selection to guide people seamlessly through the selection process and give more context for their choice.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing decision friction in the gift array for prospective donors coming from paid traffic significantly increased donor conversion. By providing fewer choices and more social proof as guidance, the cognitive momentum of these visitors improved, leading to a higher likelihood of making a donation.
The treatment version, which included a simplified gift array with amounts under $100 and highlighted the most popular selection, resulted in a 26.1% increase in donations for all traffic, with a 96.2% level of confidence. Moreover, new visitors experienced a significant 261% increase in donations, and email visitors saw a remarkable 615% increase in donations, both with a 96% level of confidence.
To further leverage these learnings in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing and optimizing the gift array for different traffic sources. By tailoring the gift options based on the visitors' affinity and previous giving history, we can further reduce decision friction and increase donor conversion.
Additionally, it would be valuable to explore other forms of social proof beyond highlighting the most popular selection. Testing different types of social proof, such as testimonials or impact statements, may provide further guidance to prospective donors and enhance their decision-making process.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of optimizing the gift array to align with the preferences and needs of different traffic sources. By minimizing decision friction and providing guidance through social proof, we can maximize conversion rates and ultimately increase the number of donations for the Leadership Institute.

experiments.title: How including gift array options in an email impacted clicks in a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a sample gift array for a direct email appeal will achieve an increase in engagement because there will be additional cognitive momentum to continue the process to the next step.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2023 - 07/13/2023
In a high urgency campaign, we typically keep the decisions isolated per each step. In the email step, we help the reader decide “Do I want to click?” and then at the donation page step we help the reader decide “How much do I want to give?” In one email, we wanted to test if adding gift array options at the email level would help more people begin thinking about both questions at once. The treatment version added a simple line of copy, letting the reader know that donations of all sizes and amounts were needed and welcomed and would qualify toward the campaign goal: “Whether you give $10, $100, $1,000 or more, every donation counts.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we learned by running this experiment is that adding a sample gift array in the email increased engagement: The treatment version, which included a line of copy mentioning various donation amounts, resulted in a 23.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that introducing a gift array at the email level can help in generating additional cognitive momentum and increasing engagement. We also believed this may have motivated more people who were considering making a gift but did not have all of the information needed to move forward with the decision, by reminding them that they could have the autonomy if they wanted to choose to give a gift of $10 or $100 and that all gifts would be counted.
Based on these learnings, future experimentation should consider incorporating sample gift arrays or suggested donation amounts in email appeals. This can potentially encourage donors to consider different giving options and increase their likelihood of clicking on the donation link. Additionally, these findings may also be useful in exploring the impact of dynamic gift arrays in other communication that are based on previous giving history—a tactic often used in direct mail letters that we could test applying in the digital space.

We will also want to measure downstream conversion and lifetime value of these $10 donors and overall retention rate compared to other donors.

We were running a survey to an audience of lapsed donors and followed the survey with an instant donation page. In previous testing, we had found that asking for a $10 gift in the body of the email copy increased donor conversion rate. We were interested in testing this same concept at the donation page level and hypothesized that reducing the number of choices on the gift array to only ask for a $10 gift would increase conversion rate from these lapsed donors. experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed after running the experiment for 2 1/2 weeks was that there was a 79.8% increase in conversion rate with 99.5% level of confidence. We believe this is due to the reduced friction in the treatment experience where the donor did not have to pause and decide on an amount to give they could just decide "Do I want to give $10? Yes or no?" and continue their cognitive momentum from answering questions on the survey. We made the choice simple, reducing the perceived barrier in the decision-making process. While this treatment produced a better response in reactivating lapsed donors, in future testing we would like to test how this might impact new donor conversion as well. We also want to test increased gift amounts (such as $15 or $20) on the single gift array option to identify if it is the single choice or the lower amount that impacts results.

experiments.title: How adding a $60 giving option to the Lamplighters monthly gift array impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a third monthly giving option as an “in-between” amount  for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations and revenue as it will reduce giving friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/19/2022 - 11/19/2022
The PFI Lamplighters program gives people the opportunity to support prisoners in ways that help them become closer to God. The current monthly giving options for Lamplighters are $15 to support one prisoner and $165 to support an entire class. We hypothesized that adding a middle amount as a giving option would reduce giving friction, giving donors an additional option that might nestle in nicely between an amount that is too low or not impactful enough and an amount that is too high.
To test this hypothesis, we added a $60 monthly giving option to support four prisoners.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the $60 giving option to the monthly giving gift array resulted in a 79.3% lift in revenue with a 97.6% level of confidence. Donations saw an 11.8% decrease but only with a 54.4% level of confidence.
The impressive increase in revenue tells us that users responded very well to seeing a third giving option between $15 and $165. The $60 option reduced decision friction to where the donor did not have to think of an amount to settle in between the extremes of $15 and $165 - instead, the option was presented to them! Additionally, the $60 option will bring up our average monthly gift.
It's worth noting that new visitors produced a 114% lift in revenue with a 98.3% level of confidence. This tells us that people that are less familiar with the Lamplighters program appreciate the $60 giving option, as they're likely too new to the program to be willing to make a $165 monthly donation. But they are willing to commit $60/month to the program.

experiments.title: How the gift frequency section on a primary donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the way the gift frequency section was presented and functioned for prospective donors will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2021 - 02/12/2021
Focus on the Family recently changed their donation platform. During that transition, the way the gift frequency section was presented looked and functioned differently than before. They heard from prospective donors that it was slightly confusing for them as they made a gift. To test into the best approach for that section on the page, we developed two different variations in hopes that we could increase clarity to the giving process. The first variation presented the two different gift frequency options as checked boxes – requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted to make through the checked boxes. The second variation presented the options as buttons. Again, requiring the prospective donor to have to select the gift they wanted by selecting the specific button. This experiment originally ran during the last few weeks of their calendar year-end campaign and showed a negative impact on donor conversion from both variations. We proposed testing this again outside of year-end, during neutral motivation.
experiments.keyLearnings: Similar to when we tested these variations at year-end, there was a negative impact on donor conversion from both treatments. In fact, the most significant decrease (16%) in donor conversion came from the button variation. The checked boxes also saw a 10% dip in donations as well. This experiment is a difficult one to fully understand because the two variations that were developed were intended to bring clarity to the giving process - something that donors had communicated that they needed. Instead, we added friction to the giving process. We hypothesize that the current control requires no work at all. In fact, We think that donors don't really notice it or understand it so they just continue to progress down the giving form to make their gift. The two new variations may be making the donor pause and think about the action they want to take when they get to that section. Continued testing needs to be done to: 1) gain greater clarity to the motivation of the donor and elements of friction on the page 2) explore our hypothesis about the gift frequency section altogether. To do this, our next test is going to be to completely remove the "Choose your gift frequency" section and present a recurring option in a different way.

experiments.title: How decreasing the ask amount on a content offer instant donation page impacted conversions (physical resource)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the gift array  for new names acquired will achieve an increase in transactions because we have decreased the perceived sacrifice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2022 - 10/15/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that has physical resources to acquire new names through Facebook and website traffic.
When a resource is requested, the individual completes a form to receive the item and is then directed to an instant donation page.
The gift array used on these instant donation pages was $25, $50, $75, $95.
As these are brand new people to Pathway to Victory, we wanted to see if reducing the array would increase motivation to make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, while we did see a decrease in average gift, $27.24 vs $16.15, we saw an increase of 130.9% in transactions. This increase in transactions contributed to an increase of overall revenue by 37.8% with a 92% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that by providing a lower ask in the gift array, we reduced anxiety for potential new donors. As new contacts are acquired through these resources, a $25 ask can be perceived as a "risk" whereas a $10 ask is more conservative and less of a risk and sacrifice to new prospective donors.
For these donation pages, PTV does not have a minimum donation amount required therefore they experience many gifts that are $1-5. However, with this experiment, we saw a DECREASE in these low-level gifts and saw more donors willing to make a $10 gift.
Our hypothesis is that by having the ask as $10 and "risk" being decreased, then more individuals were willing to make the full donation of $10 or more rather than a donation of $1-5.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 37.8% increase with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: The effect of a reverse (high to low) gift array on donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which gift array arrangement will produce the greatest donation rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2018 - 04/02/2018
Gift arrays have always been a point of interest for fundraisers. We’ve tested different amounts, different options, different minimums. We’ve even eliminated the array in favor of an open field. What we have not tested is the order in which to present the amounts.
Many nonprofits online, across all different verticals, when using a gift array choose to show the largest suggested amount first, rather than the lowest suggested amount, likely in hopes that donors will give more.
Does this matter? Especially for CaringBridge donors, who have been quite responsive in helping us understand gift arrays to-date.
How we test this: The control (original) keeps the standard rising suggested amount approach (start with the lowest ($50) on the left (assuming visitors in this case naturally read left to right) and then end with the highest ($250)
The treatment, however, reverses this, by starting with the highest amount first in the eyepath (in this case, left).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By showing the larger amount first, visitors were LESS likely to donate, and LESS likely to give in larger amounts.
But why?
When you think about the donation array, or suggested giving amounts, it all comes down to permission; meaning, what do we, as an organization, want the reader to believe is an ACCEPTABLE amount to give, and thus completely satisfy and receive that warm glow of giving?
By starting with a higher number first, we likely gave visitors the impression that lower amounts are LESS acceptable, despite them being on the list still. (for the same reason “other” is on the list… we won’t refuse their money, but maybe they will imagine us giving them a weird/disappointed look when they do)
Thus, because those visitors perceived their smaller donation was less acceptable, they chose not to donate at all.
Now, as for the drop in average gift approaching validity, this is likely due to the potential upgrade effect that happens when amounts are suggested in that rising order. By starting low, making the low amount feel acceptable, it is possible they feel like they can share a greater feeling of warmth by giving more, whereas in the opposite condition, giving just a little bit more doesn’t seem to mean any more than giving the original amount, due to its presentation.

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/11/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They use free content offers to acquire donors online. They wanted to see if they could improve donor response by asking for less on their instant-donation page that loads after someone completes the form to claim a free eBook. Their offer donation pages all had giving arrays that started at $50. They hypothesized that $50 could seem like a lot of money to give after getting a free eBook, especially during a period of high inflation like what the US was experiencing. They wanted to see if asking for $10 would reduce mental friction and increase donor conversion.
They created a new version of the donation page for their Praying Scriptures for Your Child devotional eBook and split the traffic 50/50. They ran the experiment for one month and saw the following results.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 4 weeks, we did not have greater than 50 gifts in the sample size, but we did validate a significant lift on donor conversion. We believe that, during periods of high inflation like we are experiencing right now, asking for less, especially in acquisition offers, reduces mental friction and makes it easier for someone to give their first gift. We will continue to test this in their future offers when inflation cools down to see if we can ask for a higher gift amount without harming our donor acquisition rates.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array options for an acquisition offer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array options for people who downloaded a study guide will achieve a higher donor conversion rate on the instant donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2022 - 10/17/2022
Luther Seminary has been running an acquisition offer on Facebook for a free study guide for the book of Proverbs. The ICR was averaging around 0.8% in the months leading up to this experiment, so we wanted to test what lowering the gift array would do for people downloading the study guide and then being redirected to the instant donation page.
As you can see in the images below, the gift array options ranged very large and high for an offer with low value that was ultimately being presented to new names and potentially new donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The lower gift array resulted in a 638.5% increase in donor conversion rate with a 97.8% LoC. We believe this is because the value of the content offer received was not equal to the value of donation options given on the original donation page. The options presented in the treatment were far more tangible to someone who is brand new coming onto the Luther Seminary file as a majority of our samples were new site visitors.
While running this experiment for the Proverbs study guide, we also ran the same experiment for a Revelation study guide and Revelation online course. The results we've seen in these experiments were the opposite as what has been presented for this experiment. Although they have not reached a significant level of validation, we're seeing directional decreases in donor conversion for both experiments.
We believe this is because of how people view the level of complexity and perceived value each offer (and specifically the book of the Bible) provides to the consumer.
Furthermore, we'd like to continue to test this concept on new acquisition offers to see how best that audience responds.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
New visitors had a 534.8% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array options for an acquisition offer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array options for potential donors who just signed up for an online course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate on the instant donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2022 - 10/20/2022
Luther Seminary has been running an acquisition offer on Facebook for a free online course for the book of Revelation. The other acquisition offers they are offering are free study guides for the Book of Proverbs and Book of Revelation, so this online course was something different and new to give to their audience.
The performance of this online course shortly after launching it was very good with the ICR averaging at 4.7% and the GCPD averaging at $110.
We’ve been testing the lower gift array on both study guide funnels since the control gift array seems to be a high ask for an offer with a lower value. We decided to test this same concept on this online course since, historically, online courses are perceived to provide more value to potential donors.
We wanted to see if this audience would react any differently to seeing a lower gift array when offered a higher value free resource.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The lower gift array did not have an effect on donor conversion rate as we saw a 13.1% decrease in conversion rate with only a 43.3% LoC. Although, the lower gift array did have a large impact on average gift resulting in a 45.4% decrease in revenue with a 97.6% LoC.

We believe this happened because the donors on the control page saw the value of this online course therefore the lowest ask of $50 did not sway them from giving a gift. But the donors who saw the treatment page were more likely to give a smaller gift of $25.

We now can assume that donors are more likely to give higher gifts when presented the option to do so when the offer they received is of high value.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:

experiments.title: How refining the donation value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition focused on the resource center improve conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2015 - 11/18/2015
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a resource center for biblical perspectives on today’s issues. The goal of this resource center was email and donor acquisition. Immediately after the signup page was a donation ask.
DTS had run a similar value proposition experiment on this page before but had seen no significant difference in results. Taking those learnings, they did a more radical test. They tested which value proposition was stronger: an ask for an investment in a student or an ask supporting the provision of the resource center. They also changed the placement of the endorsement and added a photo of the endorser.
experiments.keyLearnings: DTS learned that messaging and layout can have significant impact on donor conversion rate and average gift. By having a stronger value proposition that supported the resource center coupled with reducing friction by having the endorsements inline with the rest of the body copy, they were able to see a 553.3% lift in conversion rate and a lift in average gift of 241.4%. This is also an indicator of the value proposition being reinforced in the messaging and having suggested giving amounts instead of an open field.

experiments.title: How the minimum premium gift impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase overall revenue by increasing the minimum gift required for a donation premium?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2016 - 06/23/2016
On one of their donor acquisition campaign, The Heritage Foundation was testing the use of a premium to increase conversion and average gift. Previous experiments had proven that the premium increased the likelihood of a donor to convert but we wanted to see if we could also increase to overall revenue coming from the campaign. We decided to try testing the premium at a $125 minimum gift level. The control treatment had the minimum gift set at $100.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The $125 minimum gift was able to lift the average gift by 46% but it decreased the overall conversion by 58%. This resulted in a 39% decrease to overall revenue for the treatment. In this situation, the $100 minimum gift amount was the clear winner between the two.

However, even if this test had managed a slight increase to revenue but still decreased conversion rate significantly, the control would still have been the winner. The purpose of this campaign was new donor acquisition. It would take a significant increase in revenue to justify any loss in the number of donors acquired.

Dallas Theological Seminary launched a free online course to grow their email file and attract new donors. After a visitor signed up for the course, they were presented with an opportunity to give, accompanied by a premium offer to receive a Bible commentary.
Previous testing and optimization with DTS had shown that their donors preferred an open field instead of a gift array with recommended gift amounts. But as this course began to take off, they realized they were bringing new people to the page. They hypothesized that these new people might benefit from a choice of gift options rather than a blank field.
So they created a second page with the same copy, but three gift options above the donation form and ran an A/B split test to find out which one resulted in more conversions and donation revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the gift array produced a 34.9% lift in conversion. However, it also produced a 21% decrease in average gift. Since the decrease in average gift was less than the increase in conversion, the treatment also generated more revenue, which made it the winner.
This tells us that the “unlimited choice” of an open field produces too much decision friction in this instance. It also reminds us that as an audience grows past those who are familiar with an organization, previously tested ideas might be called into question — which reinforces the value and importance of continual testing and optimization.

experiments.title: How clarifying and reinforcing the monthly ask affected revenue per visitor
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clarifying and reinforcing the monthly ask for prospective donors will achieve an increase in monthly donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/07/2021
Prison Fellowship International wanted to increase their monthly donor conversion rate for their Lamplighter prison ministry program. In reviewing the page, they found two key opportunities to optimize conversion. First, they hypothesized the design of the “one-time” or “monthly” selector might be making it unclear to the viewer which option was selected. To attempt to make this clearer, they changed the color and added a checkbox to increase visual certainty as to which option was selected.
Then, in an attempt to increase the percentage of donors who selected a monthly gift, they added an image and a text callout that reinforced the benefits of giving a monthly gift for the organization and for the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for several weeks, the treatment directionally increased recurring donor conversion, but the result was not significant or statistically valid.
However, looking at overall donations, there was a 21.7% increase in all giving, and a noticeable lift in average gift, even after large outlier gifts were removed. When revenue per visitor was calculated, it emerged that the combination of increased conversion and increased average gift led to a 134.6% increase in revenue.
The big question is why did this happen?
Our hypothesis is that clarifying whether "one-time" or "monthly" was selected increased conversion by giving more clarity to the visitor as to their gift type. This may also have had an impact on average gift as one-time donors (who typically give larger gifts) were more confident that their gift was, in fact, one-time.  Additionally we hypothesize that the "impact language" designed to increase recurring gift conversion increased overall average gift by inserting value proposition into transactional donation form, right at the moment before the donor selected how much they were going to give.
The next test will focus on providing separate gift arrays for "one-time" and "monthly" selections with the goal of increasing average gift for one-time gifts.

experiments.title: How a higher suggested ask amount affects conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the ask amount for journal visitors will achieve an increase in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2021 - 04/23/2021
CaringBridge’s tribute widget had a $30 ask, which they had calculated as the amount of money that it takes to keep one site running for a full month. Interestingly, the amount wasn’t reflected on the following donation page — and when that amount was added, it reduced conversion. They wanted to test the opposite—bringing the suggested amount from the following donation page into the tribute widget. To do this, they suggested an ask of $50. They hypothesized that this might decrease conversion, but that they might make up the decrease in additional average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This increased suggested ask produced an 11.8% decrease in conversion rate. This might be acceptable, but the increase in average gift was only $4, which did not offset the revenue lost from the reduction in donations.
The ideal outcome would have been a more marginal decrease in conversion rate, as more revenue is not always an even trade for more donors. When you get more donors, you have more people you can renew or upgrade. However, donors who give at higher amounts are often able to give much more over their lifetime with the organization.
This is a perfect example of why we test—to discover risks and tradeoffs without making drastic changes.
