experiments.title: How simplifying the gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying the gift array increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/23/2016 - 09/02/2016
Heritage Action created a “Proud Conservative” window decal to offer as an incentive to donors. However, when they launched it, they didn’t see the kind of response they were expecting. They had quantified the gift array to show how many window decals the donor would receive for each amount. But they hypothesized that this might be suppressing conversion by making the transaction too complex.
They created a treatment with just the dollar amounts on the gift buttons. That way, the donor had to make one decision: “how much do I give?” without the additional decision of “how many decals do I want?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The simplified gift array produced a 94% increase in donor conversion, as well as an increase in average gift. This was an important ripple effect to the learning—looking at the distribution of gifts, most people just wanted one decal, which led them to select the lowest option.
When the relationship between gift amount and the decals was removed, people were free to select their gift amount regardless of the number of decals they wanted, which proved to increase the average gift.

experiments.title: How a higher preselected value on a gift array impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would increasing the minimum gift and changing to a higher preselected gift array amount increase donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2019 - 11/26/2019
In a continued effort to improve average gift in their acquisition efforts, TFAS looked to create a new experience that increased the minimum gift array value from $25 to $50, then chose to apply their “Most Popular” gift array treatment that was tested into that improved donor conversion and revenue previously and move it from the $50 value to the $75 amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 97% level of confidence, we validated a decrease of 67.9% in revenue from this gift array treatment.
This decrease in revenue was really driven by a combination of decreasing donor conversion (which was -55.3%, LoC: 92%) along with a smaller average gift.
An additional interesting observation is that between the two preselected values across the treatments shown the following was true:
For the control experience, 50% of the gifts we received in the experiment were at or above the “Most Popular” gift level of $50. Whereas,
The treatment experience produced just 16% of the gifts at or above the “Most Popular” gift amount of $75.
With such a small sample, it’s hard to conclusively confirm this, but this leads to suspicion that at least in acquisition, when we ask for a smaller amount up front, that people are inclined to be more generous, whereas if we ask for too much on the first gift, they say “no” altogether.
Conclusion:
For this reason, we have concluded that at least with acquisition, that the combination of starting the gift array at $50 and preselecting $75 as the “Most Popular” gift amount appears to be “too high of a floor” for this audience. Further experimentation should be made with other donation forms, but this combination certainly appears to not be the best approach for acquisition.

experiments.title: Determining the “price elasticity” of a recurring gift ask amount
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the minimum donation amount of the recurring gift prompt from $15 to $25 significantly increase overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2019 - 02/26/2019
Focus on the Family had seen significant success acquiring new recurring donors through the popup recurring gift ask. The popup was a window that would appear, prior to a gift being processed, that asked the donor to convert their one-time gift into a recurring gift. We had done several iterations of experiments on the value proposition used on the prompt but seen limited success in increasing the conversion rate. With this in mind, we decided to instead see if we could increase the average recurring gift amount by increasing the minimum donation ask. We were trying to determine the price elasticity of the minimum gift ask.
In this situation, we would potentially lower the donor conversion rate but lift overall revenue by significantly increasing average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We reduced the overall recurring donor acquisition coming from the recurring gift popup by 49% when we increased the minimum gift ask to $25. It was expected that we would see some level of decrease so we dug deeper into the data to determine the overall impact on revenue. When we did, even though we saw a 66% increase to average gift, we still see an overall 26% decrease in revenue from the increased minimum gift amount.
As a note, for the purpose of this analysis, we only included donors in the sample size and conversion metrics if they were giving a small enough gift to be impacted by the reduced gift amount ($40 or less).

experiments.title: The effect of a reverse (high to low) gift array on donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which gift array arrangement will produce the greatest donation rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2018 - 04/02/2018
Gift arrays have always been a point of interest for fundraisers. We’ve tested different amounts, different options, different minimums. We’ve even eliminated the array in favor of an open field. What we have not tested is the order in which to present the amounts.
Many nonprofits online, across all different verticals, when using a gift array choose to show the largest suggested amount first, rather than the lowest suggested amount, likely in hopes that donors will give more.
Does this matter? Especially for CaringBridge donors, who have been quite responsive in helping us understand gift arrays to-date.
How we test this: The control (original) keeps the standard rising suggested amount approach (start with the lowest ($50) on the left (assuming visitors in this case naturally read left to right) and then end with the highest ($250)
The treatment, however, reverses this, by starting with the highest amount first in the eyepath (in this case, left).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By showing the larger amount first, visitors were LESS likely to donate, and LESS likely to give in larger amounts.
But why?
When you think about the donation array, or suggested giving amounts, it all comes down to permission; meaning, what do we, as an organization, want the reader to believe is an ACCEPTABLE amount to give, and thus completely satisfy and receive that warm glow of giving?
By starting with a higher number first, we likely gave visitors the impression that lower amounts are LESS acceptable, despite them being on the list still. (for the same reason “other” is on the list… we won’t refuse their money, but maybe they will imagine us giving them a weird/disappointed look when they do)
Thus, because those visitors perceived their smaller donation was less acceptable, they chose not to donate at all.
Now, as for the drop in average gift approaching validity, this is likely due to the potential upgrade effect that happens when amounts are suggested in that rising order. By starting low, making the low amount feel acceptable, it is possible they feel like they can share a greater feeling of warmth by giving more, whereas in the opposite condition, giving just a little bit more doesn’t seem to mean any more than giving the original amount, due to its presentation.

experiments.title: How increasing the one-time gift array on a Meta donation form impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing gift arrays for Meta donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2025 - 12/10/2025
This test evaluated the effect of increasing the one-time ask array on Meta forms. Our hypothesis was that keeping the end anchor amount of $150 but increasing the amounts between would increase average gift without negatively impacting conversion. We had previously tested the one-time pre-selected gift array for Meta; the test results can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to determine if increasing the middle amounts within a one-time ask array on Meta forms, while keeping the $150 end anchor, would increase the average gift value without decreasing conversion rates.
What Drove the Lift
Most donors default to the pre-selected amount, so the gains were concentrated among active choosers — donors who navigated away from the default. Two dynamics appear to have influenced their behavior:
The compromise effect: Donors tend to avoid extremes and gravitate toward a middle option. Raising the middle amounts nudged that "safe" choice upward.
 	Reduced sticker shock: Tighter gaps between amounts made the transition from lower to higher options feel more natural.
Next Steps
We'll be rolling out the variant and continuing to test — both around the specific amounts and which value is pre-selected as the default.

experiments.title: Content Offer 2: Can adjusting gift arrays and removing preselection improve donor acquisition for paid social content offers?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom and depends on donor support to sustain its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover is testing donation form configurations that balance donor acquisition with gift value, particularly for newer content offers distributed through paid media.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how gift array size and preselection affect conversion and revenue outcomes. The test combined two gift arrays ($25/50/100 and $50/100/250) with and without preselecting the second gift amount, resulting in four variants. This specific read focuses on the “Nine Myths about Capitalism” content offer, which was newly launched and distributed primarily through Meta during the test period.
Note: this is a drilldown of a larger experiment (link).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
For the “Nine Myths about Capitalism” offer, results were mixed and not statistically valid, but directional patterns were consistent across variants.
📊 Performance metrics
Total samples: 1,445Total conversions: 52Overall conversion rate: 3.6%Overall average gift: $38.64Control ($50/100/250, no select):Conversion rate: 3.4%Average gift: $52.50$25/50/100, no select:Conversion rate: 5.2% (+56.6% vs. control)Average gift: $34.97Preselecting $100 on the higher array:Conversion rate: 1.3% (–60.6% vs. control)Average gift: $57.00
✂️ Offer and audience context
This was a new content offerTraffic skewed heavily toward Meta-paid acquisitionPreselecting a gift amount reduced conversion rates on both gift arraysThe $25/50/100 array without preselection leaned toward acquiring more donors, while the higher array without preselection maintained a strong average gift
The data suggests that preselection may introduce friction for this audience and offer type. While the control performed well on average gift, removing preselection—particularly on the lower array—appears more favorable for donor acquisition. Given the newness of the offer and paid social context, these signals likely reflect early-funnel donor sensitivity rather than price resistance alone.
✅ Statistical validity
Results did not reach statistical significanceConfidence levels were below the 95% threshold required for validation
Next Steps
Consider breaking out audiences for the higher gift array without preselection to evaluate whether it performs better for higher-intent segments. Alternatively, launch new offers initially with a higher array and no preselect to capture early high-value donors, then shift to a lower array configuration to optimize cost per conversion as the offer matures.

experiments.title: How changing the gift array for members on a campaign donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the gift array for non-members will achieve an increase in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2025
The Heritage Foundation ran their annual President’s Match campaign in July. In three of their emails to members, they wanted to test increasing the one-time gift amounts on the donation page to $50, $100, $250 to see if revenue would increase as well.
They changed the gift array for 50% of the traffic.
***The monthly tab’s gift array was also adjusted from $15, $25, $35 to $14, $24, $42, but we do not have the data to split apart recurring from one-time gifts, so this write up includes all transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the gift array for members did not achieve the intended increase in revenue. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in revenue of 22.8% compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at an 86.4% level of confidence.

Additionally, the treatment segment saw a 25.9% decrease in transactions with a 94.3% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of changes to donation strategies before fully implementing them. It may be beneficial to conduct smaller-scale tests or pilot studies before rolling out changes to a larger audience. Additionally, considering factors such as donor behavior, demographics, and giving preferences can help tailor fundraising strategies to better meet the needs and expectations of supporters.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing and evaluating different approaches to fundraising to ensure that the intended outcomes are achieved and to optimize revenue generation for the organization.

experiments.title: How including a gift array in a high urgency campaign overlay affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array on the pop up for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it allows the user to begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2025 - 03/12/2025
The Navigators are running a pop-up on their website as a part of their February Sustainer campaign. They wanted to test how including a gift array on the pop up would affect donor conversion rates.
They believed that more people would choose to give once getting to the donation page because the user has already begun the process of giving by selecting how much to give.
This test ran for the entirety of the campaign and was shown to 50% of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array on the pop-up for homepage visitors led to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group saw a 65.9% higher donation rate compared to the control group. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 81.9% is below the standard threshold of 95%, indicating that the results may not be entirely reliable.

Furthermore, the treatment saw a 104.1% lift in revenue at a 67.7% LOC. While neither of these metrics validated, they do suggest that The Navigators audience responded positively to this addition.

It is also important to note that the gift array buttons preselected the donation page with the amount selected on the overlay. Therefore, taking one step out of the process once the user landed on the donation page which may have been perceived as less work and friction.

Moving forward, it is essential to continue testing the effectiveness of the gift array on donation conversion rates, ensuring a robust and reliable methodology to draw conclusive insights. It would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and different variations of the gift array to validate the initial findings. Additionally, considering factors such as donor preferences and user experience could help optimize the gift array strategy for better results in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How $100 Prefill vs a $100 Pre-Select in the Array Impacts Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a $100 prefill for users on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because whereas a 3-option array seemed to introduce friction, an open $100 prefill accomplished the desired impact on average gift without introducing more options for the user to choose between..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2024 - 01/16/2025
On CaringBridge’s main donation page, $100 is the default pre-selected amount. However, how that number has been presented to the user has varied. The standard practice has been to have no gift array, but simply an open field with $100 prefilled. Alternatively, some pages utilized an array of three options with $100 as the preselected amount. It is worth noting that currently, neither of these options were present on the CaringBridge main donation page due to a migration glitch. We hypothesized that when testing between the two options, an open field with $100 prefilled (the way it was originally intended) would improve donor conversion over a 3-option array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

The treatment of using an open field with a $100 prefill on the main donation page resulted in a 35.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence was 92.0%, indicating a high likelihood that the increase in revenue was due to the treatment and not random chance. Furthermore, the hypothesis that a $100 prefill would improve donor conversion rates compared to a 3-option array was supported by the results of the experiment.

Ultimately, these results help enforce two next steps for action items. 1. That CaringBridge should consider testing their other donation pages which have a gift array by default, to see whether an open field pre-fill can also improve conversion rate. 2. CaringBridge should prioritize correcting the current main donation page so that the $100 prefill is represented, rather than the current blank open field (0 is the prefill)

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array options on a membership donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who are activating their membership will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/09/2024 - 08/07/2024
An organization has a donation page where people can make a donation of $25 or above and activate their membership. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $50, $100, and $250.
The treatment’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment is that decreasing the gift array amounts did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate: Contrary to the hypothesis, the treatment group, which had lower gift options of $25, $50, and $100, experienced a 23.7% decrease in total transactions for all traffic compared to the control group, which had gift options of $50, $100, and $250. This decrease was at 83.8% which, while not valid, is directional.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to analyze the impact of the language used in the gift array on donor behavior. It is possible that the specific wording and formatting of the gift array may have influenced donor decision-making in ways that were not captured by this experiment.

Overall, it is essential to carefully design and execute experiments, take into account all relevant factors, and analyze results with a critical eye before making any decisions based on the findings.

experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form gift array button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s gift array and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2024 - 05/30/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
The Heritage team wondered whether this flag image would appeal to potential donors on another direct appeal donation page. They believed testing this image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, due to both the image’s appeal to the donors’ patriotism and the suggestion of $100 as the “preselected” array option.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon only in the $100 gift array button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's gift array button may not always lead to an increase in conversion rate as expected. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic by 42.6% with an 88.3% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control performed better.
This result may indicate that the flag image was a distraction from the task at hand for this direct appeal. The image on the gift array button specifically may have confused or dissuaded some potential donors who planned on giving an amount other than the preselected amount.
In future experimentation, it would be important to further test the impact of adding the flag image in different elements of the donation form, such as the monthly tab as well as the submit button, or even testing different patriotic symbols or messaging to see if that resonates better with potential donors.

experiments.title: How adding an impact callout above the gift array impacted average gift and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact callout to the gift array for visitors on the confirmation donation pages will achieve an increase in average gift because the callout will incentivizing and highlighting a larger gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2024 - 05/23/2024
Focus on the Family is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. After someone signs up for a free video series, they’re brought to a confirmation page that asks if they will make a gift to share the series with others. These confirmation pages also promote a physical book premium offer as a thank-you for donating.
In reviewing these confirmation pages, we realized that the body copy on the page was asking for a specific gift amount — usually $30 or $50 — so that the reader can get the premium book offer. However, the gift array did not have this specific amount highlighted in any way.
We decided to run an experiment to test whether adding a callout that highlights a desired gift amount and reminds the reader of their gift’s impact would increase average gift amounts and overall donor conversion.
Our theory was that the impact callout would increase average gift by highlighting a higher default gift amount in the array. We also theorized that the impact statement would increase donor conversion by reminding the reader of the impact their gift would have on other families looking for resources like the video series they just signed up for.
We built this test in VWO and ran it as a strict A/B test across four video series confirmation pages. The Control did not have any changes to its design. The Treatment version highlighted the second value in the gift array and also added the impact callout.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an impact callout to the gift array on confirmation donation pages could potentially increase the average gift amount and revenue. The Control had an average gift of $38.26, while the Treatment had an average gift of $41.70. This increase in average gift translates to a 9.0% lift, but it only achieved a 90.0% level of confidence (LOC).

However, while the average gift saw a positive trend, the impact callout Treatment led to significant decreases in donor conversion. We saw that the Treatment led to a -17.7% decrease in overall donor conversion with a 98.6% LOC. This trend was primarily driven by new visitors on mobile devices; returning visitors and desktop users did not see any changes in donor conversion, but they made up a minority of the test traffic.

Based on these results, we can't consider the impact callout as a validated strategy for increasing average gift. But the 90.0% LOC on lifting the average gift is promising and can be tested into further. It would be worth testing the impact callout separately from the highlighted amount in the gift array to see which element of the Treatment design led to the increase in average gift and decrease in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve more revenue because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake can significantly increase revenue. The treatment group saw a 40.2% increase in revenue for all traffic, which indicates that providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used can motivate donors to give more.
However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 82.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that while the results show a positive impact, they are not completely reliable and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of gift handles and impact descriptions in donation appeals to see if the results can be replicated and validated. Additionally, it may be helpful to investigate other factors that could influence donor behavior and revenue generation to further optimize fundraising efforts for Eagle Lake.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options on a gift array impacted non-donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of options on a gift array for nondonors will achieve an increase in conversion because there are fewer options to consider thereby reducing decision friction in the donation process. because  there are fewer options to consider thereby reducing decision friction in the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/09/2024
We wondered if having a $200 option on the gift array for nondonors was causing friction in the decision making process. We set up a test removing the highest amount from the gift array to help the donation ask feel more accessible to someone who has never made a gift to CTF before and reduce the decision friction by having too many options to consider.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by reducing the number of options on a gift array for non-donors on a post-survey donation page, there was a 14.4% directional increase in donations for all traffic at 91% level of confidence. This suggests that having fewer options to consider can reduce decision friction in the donation process and potentially increase conversion rates.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the impact of simplifying the donation process by offering fewer options on the gift array. Further testing is necessary to confirm the results and determine if this approach consistently leads to higher donation rates. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations in donation amounts to see which ones resonate best with potential donors and drive the highest conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline for visitors will achieve an increase in our donor conversion rate because it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page. because  it will provide clarity on their next steps and encourage them to continue reading down the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate on our Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a continuation headline test. The new headline reads: “Your 18-month calendar will be mailed to your address shortly. Before you go, would you consider something?”
The continuation headline will help to encourage visitors to read further down the page, enabling them to read more value proposition, and then finally see the donation page.
By removing “Thank you” from the headline, we believe less people will perceive the donation page as the final step in the process and rather, make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a continuation headline on the Prayer Calendar funnel led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic at a 73.8% lift. This result suggests that the continuation headline effectively communicated the next steps to the visitors and avoided the perception that their contribution was complete.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that while the results are promising, further testing is necessary to validate the findings and ensure that the continuation headline consistently generates positive results.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the continuation headline to gather more data and increase the statistical level of confidence. Additionally, it may be valuable to explore other variations in the headline to identify the most effective messaging for visitor conversion.
Overall, the findings of this experiment provide valuable insights for optimizing the Prayer Calendar funnel, but further testing and refinement are required to cement the effectiveness of the continuation headline.

experiments.title: How a Lower Suggested Gift and Array Impacts Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array and reversing it to start low to high for users on the Designed to the Core premium will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lowered “entry level” will appeal to a wider variety of donors versus excluding those who may not be ready to give a $500 gift..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2023 - 07/03/2023
RTB’s donation pages go from highest to lowest, starting at a high of $500 and then decreasing to the suggested gift of $50. We hypothesized that reversing this array to go from low to high as well as lowering the four amounts will improve the donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which involved lowering the gift array and reversing it to start low to high, showed a significant increase in donor conversion rate compared to the control version.
2. The treatment resulted in a 163.2% increase in donations for all traffic.
3. The statistical level of confidence reached 88.1%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but are not yet considered fully valid.
4. Further testing would be necessary to confirm if the treatment has the intended effect with a higher level of confidence.
Based on these learnings, it is clear that the changes made in the treatment version resonated with donors. The lower entry level and the reversed order of the gift array likely appealed to a wider variety of potential donors, attracting those who may not have been ready to give a $500 gift.
To continue experimenting in the future, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to further validate the effectiveness of the treatment. This could involve increasing the sample size, extending the duration of the experiment, or implementing additional variations to the gift array. By doing so, the organization can gain more confidence in the impact of these changes and make informed decisions about optimizing its donation pages to maximize donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2023 - 04/28/2023
Historically, Care Net has typically run a sticky bar that dynamically populates the number of donors needed across their website during high urgency campaigns. This year, we wanted to test and see how adding a gift array to this would impact donor conversion to have the amount selected happen at this level instead of when they are redirected to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner led to a significant decrease in donor conversion for all traffic, with a 47.1% decrease.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this decrease is 91.1%, which means that while it is not yet considered valid, it is approaching validity.

3. Returning visitors also saw a significant decrease in donations by 862% with a 98% level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the addition of a gift array to the home page sticky banner did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion. It actually had a negative impact on donations, particularly for female visitors and returning visitors.

In future experimentation, it would be important to further investigate why the gift array had this negative impact and to explore alternative approaches that may be more effective in increasing donor conversion. This could involve conducting user research to better understand why certain segments of visitors were deterred from donating and using these insights to inform the design of future experiments.

It is also important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This indicates that the results may not be statistically significant and further testing is necessary to draw more conclusive findings. Therefore, we'd like to test this again during their fiscal year-end campaign in June.

experiments.title: How low-to-high gift array affected donor conversion (Adam and Eve)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a low-to-high gift array for users on the Adam and Eve instant donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting a more entry-level gift amount will help increase first time donors and those unable to gift a large amount but who want to support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 06/05/2023
Reasons to Believe orders their gift arrays from highest ask to lowest; the assumption being that starting with the larger gift will increase the average gift. However, we hypothesized that for instant donation pages on offers specifically—where perhaps a user isn’t already familiar with RTB— a $250 gift might be a daunting place to start. We believe that lowering the barrier to entry will increase the number of gifts. This will lead to an overall increase in revenue even if the average gift is lowered due to the increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that offering a low-to-high gift array for users on the Adam and Eve instant donation page did not increase donor conversion rate, as hypothesized. In fact, there was a 69.8% decrease in Revenue for All Traffic with a 93.3% statistical level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the audience and context of the donation page. It may be that for certain causes or types of donors, a low-to-high gift array is not effective, but for others, it may be more successful. Therefore, further testing is needed, with a focus on identifying the specific factors that lead to successful donation pages. Additionally, it may be helpful to test multiple variations of gift arrays to determine the most effective approach for each audience and context.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options in a gift array impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the gift array to match the copy callouts for Eagle Lake supporters will achieve an increase in gifts because you eliminate the friction of multiple-choice and better highlight the $150 amount called out in the page copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2023 - 05/12/2023
For Navigators’ Eagle Lake campaign, much of the language and copy focused on how a gift helps send kids to camp. The donation page called out the amount of $150 specifically, as what’s needed to send one child to camp this year. The Navigators hypothesized that by eliminating the number of choices in the gift array to either the $150 amount or $75 (while still keeping an option for other) would increase the average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 4% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 29%. This means that the reduced number of options in the gift array had virtually no impact in people's motivation to give or not. The treatment version of the page, which only had options for $75 and $150, resulted in a 19.0% directional decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 86%. Our hypothesis is that because the control version that included $25 and $300, there were people who were inclined to give the larger amount when presented the option.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test the impact of different copy callouts and messaging on the donation page or how the gift array is presented from a design standpoint (having a preselected amount).

experiments.title: How increasing the ask amount on an instant donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by increasing the “ask amount” for online course enrollees will achieve an increase in average gift and revenue for this page because there will be a larger average gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
This theological seminary client offered a new biblical resource, the Revelation Online Course. The control donation page for the Revelation Online Course had a $10 donation ask in the copy. The treatment page had the original control donation page copy but also included a larger gift amount ask of a $50 or more donation. This treatment page experiment was set up to show to 50% of viewers as an A/B test. We wanted to test if asking for a larger gift amount in the treatment donation page could increase the average gift and revenue for this online course.
We have noticed in past experiments that including specific donation ask copy can produce a lift in conversion rate. We thought that this donation page copy for this new biblical resource would be a great place to ask specifically for a larger donation of $50 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a specific ask amount of $50 or more to the donation page copy for the Revelation Online Course, did not lead to an increase in average gift and revenue. In fact, the treatment page saw a 56.1% decrease in conversion rate. While the statistical sample size is approaching validity, the level of confidence was 88% which did not reach above 95% to validate this experiment. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to explore alternative ways of encouraging larger donations, such as providing more details on the course they will get if they donate $50 or more.

experiments.title: How a lower ask amount affects revenue on a Wikipedia-style article appeal during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a lower recurring donation ask on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal banner will achieve greater revenue by offering a lower commitment, thus increasing donations enough to offset the lower ask amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2023 - 03/24/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on recurring donor acquisition, we wanted to know if lowering the ask amount on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal would increase recurring donor rates by offering a lower commitment given that donors would be giving on a monthly basis.
This test followed on the heels of a previous experiment (during the same campaign), where we learned that the Wikipedia-style appeal tactic outperformed both a banner ad and a slide-in appeal when presented on article pages being viewed by prospective donors.
We set this up as an A/B test with roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $30 ask and roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $15 ask in the hopes that donor volume would outweigh the lower ask and provide a net increase in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 5 days, we were unable to validate our hypothesis that a lower ask amount would lift donations enough to overcome the lower ask and produce a net increase in revenue over an assumed 11-month period.
The treatment was only able to produce one additional recurring gift and the 48% decrease in revenue (81.4% level of confidence) did not justify continuing to run this experiment. Based on these results, we elected to run the $30 ask for the remainder of the campaign since it appeared that the decreased ask amount did little to increase the volume of donors while cutting overall revenue nearly in half.

experiments.title: How an ascending gift array affects revenue on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an ascending gift array for potential donors will achieve higher revenue per donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2021 - 08/27/2021
RTB has typically had a gift array on their donation pages with descending amounts, meaning the largest amount is the first option on their donation page. On RTB’s main donation page, we wanted to test reordering the gift array, updating it from decreasing amounts to increasing amounts. We wanted to see how it impacted revenue – would it lead people to give higher amounts? And how would it impact overall giving?
experiments.keyLearnings: Results indicated a 46.9% directional decrease in revenue (level of confidence 88%). While a typical gift array is set up in an ascending order, RTB's audiences seem to respond more to the descending order already put in place. Although the results are not statistically valid, we did see a directional  decrease after a month of testing. It is possible that RTB's audiences are already conditioned to respond to their gift array order. When observing donor conversion rate there is even less statistical significance (10% difference at 50% level of confidence). It is recommended that this is retested on the instant donation pages of offers as those funnels tend to have more newer users which may respond differently than RTB's current audience.

experiments.title: The Impact of a Higher Suggested Gift Array on a Premium Offer Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by slightly increasing the suggest gift amounts on the donation page’s giving array, we will increase revenue for FamilyLife’s premium offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2022 - 04/25/2022
FamilyLife uses premium offers as their primary acquisition source for new donors on Facebook. Their most popular offer is the Seeing Beautiful Again book that is given with a donation to the ministry. The vast majority of traffic to this donation page is from new visitors who saw an ad and clicked through to the page. We hypothesized that a slightly higher suggested gift amount on the giving array would increase donor revenue. We increased each option by $5.
NOTE: In the results below, the treatment was mislabeled as “lower” array when it was in fact higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were not able to reach a greater than 95% level of confidence for any of the visitor segments during the test, however we were able to achieve the highest confidence on our new visitors audience, the largest audience for the premium. New Visitors gave 22% more revenue on average with the higher array. Returning visitors however, gave 33% les (with an 82% LOC). While this negative result for returning visitors is less reliable with its lower LOC, our hypothesis is that new visitors don't have a specific gift amount in mind when they visit. The new array serves as the MSRP for the premium and more choose the higher amount because they have no other frame of reference for how much the premium may be worth. Returning visitors, however, are likely used to "paying" less for the premium and are less likely to convert due to the "sticker shock" of the higher array.
More testing is needed to confirm whether this hypothesis is correct.  We also noticed after running the test that we preselected the $30 amount on the control but preselected the lower option ($20) on the treatment. This also could have colored the results and muddied the test. Before this treatment is used for new visitors to this or other offers, it should be tested further without a mismatch in which option is preselected.

experiments.title: How starting the gift array at a higher level impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the first gift array option for petition signers will achieve increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2021 - 02/13/2021
The Canadian Taxpayer Federation was continuing their experimentation with their gift arrays on post-petition donation forms, and decided to experiment with a higher starting point on the gift array.
The control experience started with a $10 option and the treatment started with a $25 gift option.
Would increasing the amount on the first gift array button increase the donor conversion rate and raise revenue?
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment near fully validated but was turned off early because there was a near-valid decrease in donor conversion rate of -27.5% (LoC: 93.4%).

Some other observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 54.3% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 28.0% decrease in donations with a 90% level of confidence.
The treatment decreased gifts by -83% and revenue by -85% at the $10 (or below) gift level.
 	The treatment increased gifts by +23% and revenue by 23% at the $25 gift level. And,
 	After removing a large outlier gift from the treatment, we saw that it decreased gifts by -30% and revenue by -40% at the $25+ gift level.

experiments.title: How a lower gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A lower gift array will raise donor conversion on an acquisition page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2021 - 05/27/2021
Focus on the Family had a variety of gift arrays on their acquisition donation pages. We wanted to see which array worked best for acquisition, especially since people new to Focus would be more likely to give if the ask was not too steep for a first time donor.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, most of the segments saw a directional or a valid lift in donor conversion with the lower gift array. And the lower gift array did not decrease conversion in any segments with large enough samples or any meaningful level of confidence. For instance mobile saw a 17% increase at an 88% level of confidence, while desktop saw only a 2% increase at a 13% level of confidence. All indications are that the lower gift array helps and it does not hurt any large segment.

Our hypothesis is that starting at $25 rather than $50 gives a mental ladder up to the $50 gift for the premium, rather than just starting at $50. So more people would give because a $25 gift would be easier donation for most of the page visitors to give. We expected this to be especially true to new visitors who were not as familiar with Focus on the Family and would be turned off at such a high initial donation ask. But the data suggest that primarily returning visitors saw this page and that they were also more likely to give if the array started off in a smaller dollar ask.

And, the best news is that this lower gift array did not lead to a decrease in revenue. There was a 2.3% increase in revenue per visitor at a 53% level of confidence. This tells us that with more people donating, the slight decrease in average gift (from $53 to $48) did no harm to the overall revenue. So we will be rolling this gift array out to all acquisition pages and recommend it is rolled out to Housefile donation pages as well.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Referral visitors had a 33.8% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 17.5% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 150.8% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 12.4% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 38.4% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.
 	45-54 visitors had a 3.1% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 10.4% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a pre-selected value on the gift array impacted donor conversion rate and revenue from acquisition forms
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “most popular” pre-selected value on the gift array impact donor conversion rate or revenue for acquisition forms?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2020
Americans for Prosperity was looking into ways to increase their donor conversion rate and revenue from the investment they were making in acquiring donors through instant donation forms online. In reviewing the forms, we decided to try adding a “Most Popular” pre-selected option in the gift array to see how it would impact these key metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This treatment improved revenue by 94.4% (with a 92.3% level of confidence). Additionally, the donor conversion rate was showing an increase of 29.5% (although the level of confidence achieved was only 66%). Although these results didn’t officially validate, the lift in revenue is certainly directional and the decision was made to roll this out to gain the apparent benefits of the treatment option.

This increase in revenue was largely driven by an increase in the average gift amount of +55%.

Additionally, the number of $50 or greater sized gifts acquired through the treatment option was doubled in the treatment.

When donors are considering giving a gift during the instant donation page in your acquisition efforts, one of the decisions they have to make is “how much should I give?” This “Most Popular” treatment appears to decrease the friction in the process of making a donation because the donors now have a “starting point” provided to them as to make a decision about “how much” to give.

experiments.title: How inserting a gift array on a primary donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will inserting a gift array on a primary donation page affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/19/2018 - 12/04/2018
Compassion International’s main donation page featured an open field with a $100 suggested gift, and a drop down to choose whether to make the gift one-time or monthly. They were curious to see what happened when the one-time ask was replaced with a range of giving options ($50 / $100 / 250) and when the monthly recurring gift ask was brought out of the dropdown and into the decision flow as a “binary checkbox”. They created a treatment that did both of these and launched and experiment to find a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The gift array, after several months, produced an 18.5% decrease in giving, although some of this may have been due to the placement of the array—instead of being in the same position, it was moved down below the main image. However, this coincided with a 385% lift in recurring donations. Further analysis must be done to determine if the losses from one-time giving are offset by the increases to monthly giving.

experiments.title: How removing the dollar ($) sign from a gift array impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe removing the dollar sign for potential donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2025 - 07/24/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of removing the dollar sign from the ask array levels on the donation form. We hypothesized that the dollar sign signaled the users brain to enter into transactional mode rather than staying connected to the emotion of the cause. Removing the dollar sign would allow the user to stay connected to the mission and would thus increase their willingness to give, potentially at a higher level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We set out to test if the dollar sign adds friction for donors.
We thought seeing a “$” might feel transactional, deterring emotional giving. Removing it, we believed, could keep donors focused on the mission and increase giving.
What Happened:
The results told a very different story.
Instead of reducing friction, removing the dollar sign significantly hurt performance—especially on desktop. Both conversion rate and revenue per visitor dropped, in some cases dramatically.
This suggests the dollar sign isn’t a distraction. It’s actually doing something helpful: giving users clarity about what’s being asked of them.
Overall Performance:
Across all users, conversion dropped from 40.5% to 33%—a 17% decrease (52% confidence). Revenue per visitor declined even more sharply, falling 64% with 98% statistical confidence.
Desktop Performance:
The desktop saw the most dramatic impact. Conversion rate fell from 48.1% to 18%—a 62% decrease (95% confidence). Revenue per visitor dropped 75% (99% confidence).
It’s worth noting this segment had a small sample size (15 gifts), but the magnitude and consistency of the drop are hard to ignore.
Mobile Performance:
Mobile told a more mixed story. The conversion rate increased from 36.2% to 40.9% (a 13% lift), but with only 29% confidence, it is not statistically reliable.
At the same time, revenue per visitor dropped 58% (90% confidence), indicating that even when more users converted, they gave less.
One-Time Gifts:
For one-time donations, conversion declined from 32.4% to 24.2%—a 25% decrease (62% confidence). Revenue per visitor fell 69% (98% confidence), reinforcing the broader pattern: fewer gifts, and smaller ones.
Recurring Gifts:
Recurring performance showed a slight increase in conversion (8.1% to 9.1%), but with very low confidence (13%) and only 6 total gifts.
Revenue per visitor was essentially flat, decreasing just 0.97% (50% confidence). With such a small sample, these results aren’t directional.
Takeaway:
The main takeaway: keeping the dollar sign significantly improves donor clarity and response.
The dollar sign is not a barrier; it provides important clarity and guides users effectively through the donation process.
Without it, donors may feel uncertain, which can lower both participation and donation amounts.
