experiments.title: How changing the gift array for non-members on a campaign donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the gift array for non-members will achieve an increase in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2025
The Heritage Foundation ran their annual President’s Match campaign in July. In three of their emails to non-members, they wanted to test increasing the one-time gift amounts on the donation page to $50, $100, $250 to see if revenue would increase as well.
They changed the gift array for 50% of the traffic.
***The monthly tab’s gift array was also adjusted from $15, $25, $35 to $14, $24, $42, but we do not have the data to split apart recurring from one-time gifts, so this write up includes all transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the gift array for non-members from $25, $50, $100 to $ $50, $100, $250 resulted in a 1.4% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 51.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Additionally, we saw a 16.8% decrease in transactions on the treatment donation page with a 64.8% level of confidence.

Therefore, while the treatment version did show a slight increase in revenue, the lack of statistical significance means that these results should not be used for future decision-making. It's important to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes or different variations to validate the impact of changing the gift array on donation revenue.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different gift amounts and messaging to see if there are ways to further optimize donation revenue. Additionally, ensuring that experiments have a sufficient sample size and statistical significance is crucial in order to draw reliable conclusions and make data-driven decisions.

experiments.title: How increasing the gift array on a new donor acquisition page affects conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a higher gift array for new members will achieve an increase in revenue with no decrease in conversion rate because the differentials in the arrays is not significant.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/14/2025 - 06/16/2025
The Heritage Foundation had been testing on their new member acquisition page for years. At some point, the page had been altered to have a lower gift array ($25, $50, $100) than on other pages. They knew that their data showed that donors who started with a gift of $50 traditionally had higher retention rates. They wanted to test a higher gift array, with the caveat that they might reduce conversion rates overall as a tradeoff.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing a higher gift array for new members led to a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 331.0%. However, the statistical level of confidence was 0.0%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide accurate and reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to conduct experiments for a longer duration to capture any potential fluctuations over time.

Based on the results of this experiment, it would be advisable to revisit the hypothesis and potentially retest the impact of a higher gift array on new member acquisition. It is essential to gather more robust data and ensure a higher level of statistical confidence before making any changes to the current strategy. Continuous testing and learning from experiments is crucial for making data-driven decisions and optimizing performance in the long term.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio website traffic will achieve an increase in engagement and donor conversion metrics because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Radio.
This test ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
We believe that using a gift array on a sticky bar for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment testing a gift array in the lower third footer of Moody Radio's website are as follows: The one-button version resulted in a 4.6% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the standard one-button call-to-action. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was only 13.3%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for validity.

While we were unable to reach validity on this test due to the limited time frame, the results do tell us that Moody Radio's audience may be more motivated to give when there is only a single call-to-action button. On the other hand, the results suggest that the gift array version of the footer bar led to a higher average gift.

*Development team added parameters to custom transaction and revenue metrics based on the "src" associated with each variant.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Radio.
This test ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio did not lead to an increase in clicks, as initially hypothesized. In fact, we observed a 4.2% decrease in clicks across all traffic, with a statistical confidence of 36.3%. This is well below the accepted 95% threshold for validity.

Additionally, downstream metrics showed a 4.4% decrease in transactions, with a confidence level of only 13.3%.

These findings suggest that the gift array version of the footer did not effectively drive more users to the donation page and may have resulted in less motivated traffic. While these metrics did not meet the validity criteria, they point to the possibility that there may be more effective ways to encourage donations from Moody Radio listeners beyond the gift array format.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider that what may seem like a logical approach to increasing user engagement, such as allowing users to select a gift amount before reaching the donation page, may not always lead to the desired outcome. It is essential to test all changes thoroughly before implementing them on a larger scale. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are statistically sound and reach a high level of confidence before drawing conclusions.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that he gift array version of the footer  for Moody Bible website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Bible’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Bible.
This text ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for the Moody Bible website traffic showed a 4.3% increase in clicks compared to the standard one-button call-to-action. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 21.1%, which is below the 95% threshold needed to consider the results valid.
Furthermore, the treatment saw a 26.1% lift in transactions at a 43.7% LOC, meaning that the treatment led to more people engaging with the footer bar as well as led to more motivated traffic to make a gift.
However, the treatment actually resulted in a 20.7% decrease in revenue at a 56.7% level of confidence (pulled out of the number of transactions)
Control: 11 gifts, $3747
Treatment: 14 gifts, $3780
These results indicate that the treatment was more effective at getting people to engage and make a gift, but ultimately, the control produced a higher average of revenue per donor.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a statistically significant level of confidence is achieved before making decisions based on the results. This means conducting the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or making adjustments to the experiment design to improve the reliability of the results.

experiments.title: How removing gift arrays for high-value zip codes impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a gift array for potential mid-level donors will achieve an increase in average gift and conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2025 - 05/02/2025
In this experiment, we wanted to test whether removing the gift array altogether on a donation form that was being used in high-value zip codes would impact conversion rates and average gift.
The hypothesis was that by removing the ask array amounts from the form, users would not be influenced by the “suggested” amounts and would naturally input a higher amount in the open field. This would increase the average gift and the potential pool of mid-level donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we did not see any statistically-significant impact on either conversion rates or average gift for this experiment. There was a 3.5% decrease in conversion rates across the board, with a 34% level of significance, and a 2.8% decrease in revenue per conversion, with a 54% level of significance.

When breaking down the results by desktop and mobile, we also did not see any large impact:

Desktop:

Control: 30% conversion rate, $73.79 revenue per visitor

Treatment: 29.1% conversion rate (40% level of confidence) and a 2.8% decrease in revenue per visitor (54% level of confidence).

Mobile:

Control: 20.5% conversion rate, $16.72 revenue per visitor

Treatment: 4% decrease in conversion rate (25% level of confidence) and a 6.9% decrease in revenue per visitor (60% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How adding gift equivalencies to each individual ask array button impacts conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding gift equivalencies for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2025 - 06/12/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding equivalencies to the ask array section, where each gift amount would have a related ‘item’ that it could fund.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Adding gift equivalencies to the ask array section resulted in a 4.6% decrease in donations for all traffic.
 	The statistical level of confidence was only 40.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.
 	Therefore, the results of this experiment cannot be considered reliable or used for future decision making.
 	It is essential to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions from A/B experiments.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors that may have influenced the results, such as the presentation of gift equivalencies and donor preferences.
 	Further testing with a larger sample size and adjustments to the gift equivalencies may be needed to draw more accurate conclusions about their impact on conversion rates and average gift.

experiments.title: Does reducing the amount of gift arrays from 6 to 4 impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing gift array options for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because of the reduction of friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2025 - 05/15/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of reducing the number of ask array options. We hypothesized that reducing the number of options would reduce the amount of friction for the user and thus increase the conversion rate. We tested this on the main evergreen donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reducing the number of gift array options on the donation form resulted in a 20.7% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 76.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is sufficiently high before making any conclusive decisions based on the results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further investigate the impact of reducing the number of options on the donation form by conducting more tests with a larger sample size to validate these findings. This will help us make more informed decisions on how to optimize the donation process for potential donors in the future.

experiments.title: How a 3 button gift array instead of a 4 button affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a 3 button gift array for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in transactions and average gift because having a 3 button gift array with the weighted option in the middle would reduce friction and the donor would more likely make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/16/2025
Time of Grace usually has 4 gift array buttons on their donation page. For this test, we wanted to test reducing the gift array to 3 buttons and see how that would affect donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the gift array to 3 buttons on the donation page led to an 8.8% increase in transactions for all traffic. This suggests that the hypothesis that a 3 button gift array would reduce friction and increase donor conversions may have some merit. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 46.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

Therefore, while the results of this experiment are promising, they should not be used for future decision making as they are not considered statistically significant. In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing the impact of a 3 button gift array on donor conversion rates, but with a larger sample size or different experimental design to ensure more reliable and conclusive results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors such as donor preferences, messaging, and timing when designing future experiments to further optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How a gift array personalized by giving level impacts donor conversion metrics during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that raising the gift array for April Eagle Lake recipients with lifetime revenue between $100-$499 will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it better aligns with their giving capacity therefore prompting larger gift amounts.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2025
For The Navigators, April Eagle Lake campaign this year, we chose to test into gift arrays that correlated to the users giving level.
Therefore, for individuals with lifetime revenue between $100-$499, we split the recipients in half and tested the following gift arrays. Our goal was that prompting the individuals with a higher gift array would lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics.
Control: $25,$75,$150,$300
Treatment: $75,$150,$300,$450
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that raising the gift array for April Eagle Lake recipients with a lifetime revenue between $100-$499 resulted in a 43.8% increase in donations for all traffic. While this indicates a positive impact on donor conversion metrics, the statistical level of confidence was only 43.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity. These results did not validate, but they are directional.

In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a 243.9% lift in revenue at a 92.6% level of confidence. This metric did not quote reach validity, but it does show that donors were willing to make larger gifts with a higher set gift array. Furthermore, the control had an average gift of $42.50 and the treatment had an average gift of $101.67.

In the future, it is important to ensure that experiments have a higher statistical level of confidence to draw valid conclusions, but due to the small list sizes for this campaign, we were unable to reach validity. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different gift arrays or segments of donors to better understand the impact of varying gift amounts on donor conversion metrics. By conducting experiments with higher levels of confidence and testing different variables, we can gather more reliable insights to inform future fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: What happens if we add a gift array to a sticky bar?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array for the homepage sticky bar will achieve more transactions because the user will be able to start their process right there after saying yes instead of being directed to the donation page first.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
Care Net recently ran a recurring campagin to raise money for their new merger ICU.
In the past, we have tested into adding a sticky bar to the homepage. For this test, we wanted to see if we could increase transactions by adding the gift array to the sticky bar itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a 5.5% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive result, the statistical level of confidence was only 16.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

 

It is interesting to note that while we saw no difference in the total number of transactions completed, we did find a 25% lift in total revenue at a 56% LoC and a 45% lift in recurring transactions completed at a 53% LoC. While the gift array could have made this impact. I think the CTA had stake at play too and could be giving us a hint at what the audience prefers. In the control we saw a CTA of "activate my monthly gift" where as the treatment had microgoal "be 1 of 50". I think it is important to look into this microgoal to see if we can test this hypothesis out.

Overall, the experiment provided valuable insights into the potential impact of adding a gift array to the homepage sticky bar, but additional testing is needed to draw conclusive results. This information should be used to guide future experimentation and help refine strategies for increasing donations on the Care Net website.

experiments.title: How impacted gift array links in email impacts donor conversion during a high urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because it eliminates friction, pre-filling the selected amount on the donation page, while also reinforcing that their gift is being matched in the copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, we hypothesized that using impacted gift array links to drive urgency and eliminate friction during the 2-day match would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign led to a decrease in donations for all traffic. Additionally, the treatment saw a dropoff in average gift with $146 in the control versus $65 in the treatment.

Ultimately, the level of confidence was 78.9%, meaning these results are not valid for future decision-making. It would also be worth re-testing this experience for validity outside the window of a high-urgency appeal. Potentially, the treatment may have resulted in too much decision friction at the top of the funnel, whereas the links would have proved more effective simply by increasing clicks and greater traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: How a standard overlay compares to an overlay with a gift array during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an urgency overlay with an interactive gift array for site traffic during the final day of the holiday appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because the array prefills the selected amount onto the donation page, eliminating friction and providing users who are likely coming to the site in order to make their gift with a first step on page load.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2024 - 01/01/2025
During the final day of CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, half the traffic received a standard urgency overlay whereas the other half received an overlay with an interactive gift array. The frequency and amount the user selected would then be prefilled onto the donation page, eliminating friction and helping to encourage a higher gift amount. We hypothesized that the treatment would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue, providing users with a gift array on page load – assuming that much of the traffic will be coming to CaringBridge.org on the final day of the year in order to make their year-end gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included an urgency overlay with an interactive gift array, resulted in a 5.3% increase in donations for all traffic on the final day of the holiday campaign. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 48.8%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.

It's important to note that this experiment was conducted during the final day of the year, where urgency is extremely high and uniquely motivated. As seen in the little to no difference between control and treatment, it is possible that users on the site at this time already know that they intend to make their gift – meaning that whether encountering version 1 or version 2 it doesn't significantly sway that decision negatively or positively.

experiments.title: How using a gift array based on HPC impacted email donations during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a gift array based on highest previous contribution for donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. During their year-end campaign, we wanted to see whether basing a gift array on a donor’s highest previous contribution made an impact on conversion rates.
The control version had a standard gift array of $100/250/500/Other.
The treatment based the gift array on HPC, and included 1.5X HPC, 2X HPC, 2.5X HPC, and other.
This experiment was run across 3 of the last emails of the year (12/27, 12/30, and 12/31) to active and lapsed donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a gift array based on a donor's highest previous contribution can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment group in this experiment showed a 23.8% increase in donations compared to the control group, indicating that personalizing the gift array based on donor behavior may impact conversion rates positively.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was 78.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with a larger sample size to ensure more statistically significant results. It would also be valuable to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior, such as the timing of the donation appeal, messaging, or donor segmentation.

Overall, while this experiment showed a potential impact of personalized gift arrays on donation rates, further testing is needed to confirm these findings and make informed decisions for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How gift array options in a high urgency sticky bar impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the gift array to IMB’s CYE sticky bar for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift array minimizes the number of steps that need to take place on the actual donation page therefore minimizing friction and decision making resulting in more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
In an effort to boost IMB’s conversion rate from their CYE Sticky Bar, we opted to test into including a gift array on the sticky bar.
The gift array options were fully functional, and when clicked would auto fill the donation page with the applicable amount selected. We had three gift array options available on the sticky bar: $25, $75, and $250.
This test ran from Dec. 11 – Dec. 27th, 2024 with 50% of the traffic seeing the treatment version (gift array).
We hypothesized that adding the gift array would reduce friction on the donation page by minimizing the steps needed to complete a donation, ultimately resulting in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a gift array on the CYE sticky bar for homepage visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversions for IMB. In fact, the treatment version of the sticky bar with the gift array saw a 23.4% decrease in donations compared to the control group.
Despite the decrease in donations, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only at 66.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In terms of overall revenue, the control produced $15,686 and the treatment produced $10,930. Resulting in a 30.3% decrease in revenue at a 74.8% LOC.
We also looked further into the gift array amounts to see if one greatly outperformed the others.
$25 Button5 gifts$120 in Revenue$75 Button2 gifts$200 in Revenue$250 Button5 gifts$1,500 in Revenue
The remaining gifts came through the "More options" button that did not preselect a gift amount in the form meaning about half of users chose the "more options" button.
While these results are inconclusive, they do suggest that iMB's audience may react more positively to a standard sticky bar rather the gift array options.
In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and potentially tweak the gift array options or placement on the sticky bar to see if different configurations could yield more positive results. It is also crucial to ensure that statistical significance is reached before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on A/B test results.

experiments.title: How including the gift array in a sticky bar affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a gift array on a sticky bar for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rates because it simplifies the donation process, reduces friction by allowing users to select a donation amount directly from the sticky bar, and leverages psychological principles such as pre-commitment and anchoring..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2024 - 12/27/2024
The American Cornerstone Institute ran a high-urgency campaign for the final month of the year. Within this campaign, they wanted to test the impact of including the gift array on the sticky bar of their website.
They conducted an A/B test, split 50/50, with two versions of the sticky bar: the control version was a standard sticky bar with a button to take them to the donation page and the treatment version included the gift array in the sticky bar which took them to the donation page and pre-selected the amount they chose on the sticky bar.
Their hypothesis was that the gift array would increase donor conversion rate because it simplifies the donation process, reduces friction by allowing users to select a donation amount directly from the sticky bar, and leverages psychological principles such as pre-commitment and anchoring.
By making the options immediately visible and actionable, we anticipate that users will be more engaged and motivated to complete their donation, leading to higher conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Including a gift array on the sticky bar did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment version saw a 0.3% decrease in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence was only 0.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This indicates that the results of the experiment are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

- Ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. In this case, the experiment ran for 20 days, but it may have been beneficial to run it for a longer period or with a larger sample size.

- Consider other factors that may influence donor conversion rates, such as the placement of the sticky bar on the website, the wording and language used in the sticky bar, and the overall user experience on the donation page.

- Continue to test different strategies and elements on the website to optimize donor conversion rates. While this experiment did not yield the desired results, it is important to learn from it and apply those learnings to future experiments to improve the effectiveness of donation strategies.

experiments.title: Does adding a gift array to a dear-reader effect transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding a gift array to the “Dear Reader” message, instead of just a button, will result in more donations by providing clear giving options. This approach simplifies decision-making and appeals to a wider range of donors, encouraging both recurring contributions and one-time gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina NPR station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The goal of the test was to determine whether adding a gift array to a “Dear Reader” message, placed in the middle of articles, would lead to an increase in both recurring donations and overall contributions.
The body copy on both dear-readers remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment comparing the "Dear Reader" message to the "Dear Reader with Gift Array" approach showed that adding a gift array did not lead to improved performance in terms of donation rates or average gift amounts.

One-Time Donations:
The gift array version had a lower conversion rate (0.04%) compared to the standard message (0.06%), reflecting a 22.3% decrease with a 38.5% confidence level.
 	The average gift for the gift array was significantly lower ($17.25) than the standard message ($48.82).
Recurring Donations:
Both approaches yielded the same conversion rate (0.01%), showing no advantage for the gift array.
 	The average gift remained lower for the gift array ($17.25) versus the standard ($48.82).
Overall Transactions:
The gift array had a 27.4% lower conversion rate than the standard message, with a 51.0% confidence level.
 	The average gift for the gift array ($17.25) was considerably less than the standard ($48.82).

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of a quiz affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have taken a quiz will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2024 - 10/15/2024
The Heritage Foundation has an offer where people can take a quiz to test their knowledge on Marxism and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts to fight the Marxist movement in America today. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $50, $100, and $250.
The treatment’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that decreasing the gift array amounts from $50, $100, and $250 in the control group to $25, $50, and $100 in the treatment group did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group, but at a 3.6% level of confidence, this result is invalid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a statistically significant level of confidence is achieved before drawing any conclusions or making changes based on the results. This can be helped by increasing traffic to the test page. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors that could influence donor conversion rates, such as the messaging, imagery, or targeting strategies used in the donation process. Conducting further experiments with different variables and analyzing the results with a higher level of confidence will help in making informed decisions to optimize donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How does customizing a gift array based on a donor’s giving history impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that customizing the gift array for donors will achieve a higher revenue per donor because the amount is specific to their giving history.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/27/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For PTV’s year-end efforts, we decided to test the gift array on our donation page.
The control gift array for everyone is $25, $50, $100, $200
We used the donor’s HPC (highest previous contribution) or largest gift as the base for the treatment. Then we multiplied the amount by 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3.
To execute this experiment
This organization uses HubSpot as its email marketing platform and Anedot as a donation platform, but this experiment is possible with other platforms.
First, we created new calculated contact properties within HubSpot, which took the largest gift amount and multiplied it by 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3. We also had the contact property round up to the nearest dollar so the donor would not see random amounts with cents.
Second, we reviewed Anedot’s documentation on how to pass the custom amounts.
This test can still be conducted for organizations that do not have these platforms.
Use a spreadsheet to calculate the custom amounts and upload them to your email marketing platform as new fields. Then consult your donation platform documentation to see if/how to pass custom amounts.
If your donation platform does not allow custom amounts, you could break this test into buckets based on HPC. For instance, $1-50, $51-100, $101-150, $151-200, etc, or in whatever buckets best fit your organization. Then, you can AB test by sending half of the bucket to the control and half to a different donation page with a gift array that best fits that bucket’s range.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that customizing the gift array for donors based on their giving history can potentially lead to an increase in revenue per donor. In this particular case, the treatment with customized gift amounts based on the donor's highest previous contribution saw a 40.6% increase in revenue for all traffic.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 79.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
We will be conducting further analysis on this test to see how much more individuals gave based on the gift array.

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array on the instant donation page of an ebook affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that decreasing the amounts on the gift array for people who have signed up to receive an eBook will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the lower suggested gift amounts will make giving a gift feel more attainable to a larger amount of people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2024 - 09/12/2024
An organization has an offer where people sign up to receive an eBook and then are led to a donation page where they can make a gift to support the organization’s efforts. In an attempt to increase the donor conversion rate on this page, they wanted to test whether lowering the gift array amount options would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donors.
The control’s gift array was $25, $50, and $100.
The treatment’s gift array was $10, $25, and $50.
The experiment ran for one month and the control and treatment each received %50 of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that decreasing the amounts on the gift array may have a positive impact on the donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which had lower gift amount options of $10, $25, and $50, saw a 9.4% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical confidence level of 61.6% is below the threshold of 95%, meaning that these results are invalid and may not be reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide statistically significant results. It may also be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results. Additionally, it could be useful to test different variations of gift amounts to see which combination yields the best results in terms of donor conversion rates.

Overall, while the results of this experiment suggest that lowering the gift array amounts can potentially increase donor conversion rates, further testing is needed to confirm these findings and make informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How changing the gift array options for monthly gift frequency affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the gift array options for a monthly gift and moving the frequency options for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because having different options for a one-time and monthly gift will reduce their decision friction and give clarity on the type of gift and amount they want to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/19/2024
For Reasons to Believe, they typically have the same gift array options for both one-time and recurring gift frequencies. For this test, we wanted to test changing the gift array options for the monthly frequency to have options that would be more suitable for a monthly gift. We also changed the placement of the options to select the gift frequency to be above the gift array.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the gift array options for a monthly gift and moving the frequency options above the gift array did not have the desired effect of increasing donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.8% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic with a 5.9% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control group performed better.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. Therefore, it is inconclusive whether the changes made had any impact on donation behavior.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the design of the gift array options carefully and ensure that any changes made are based on solid research or data-driven insights. It may also be beneficial to conduct further testing with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the most effective approach for increasing donations.

experiments.title: Will presenting a specific ask amount on a sticky bar increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting users with a specific ask for the recurring donor drive sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be less decision friction on the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
Today in the Word recently ran a recurring donor campaign. We had previously tested into different web elements on their site during previous campaigns, but wondered if we could iterate on previous learning to try to find a new control heading into CYE.
For this test we wanted to see if listing a specific gift amount would increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting users with a specific ask for the recurring donor drive sticky bar resulted in a 59.2% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a promising result, the statistical level of confidence was only 59.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Moving forward, it is important to recognize that although the treatment showed a positive impact on donor conversion rate, the lack of high statistical confidence means that these results should not be used for future decision making at this time. It would be beneficial to conduct additional experiments with larger sample sizes or different variations to further investigate the impact of presenting a specific gift amount.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to continue testing variations of presenting specific gift amounts to donors to see if it consistently leads to increased conversion rates. Additionally, exploring other factors such as messaging, design, or timing of the ask could also be valuable in optimizing donor conversion rates for the recurring donor drive.

experiments.title: How a lower gift array impacts transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array options for donation page visitors will achieve more transactions because the amount we are asking for is less.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2024 - 08/09/2024
We were running ads for an Education Survey on Facebook for ACI and noticed that people were selecting the two lower options in the gift array, people weren’t selecting the two higher options. We decided to test a lower gift array to try to increase the amount of transactions on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that lowering the gift array options for donation page visitors resulted in a 3.0% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 20.5%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid. As a result, we should not use these findings for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to obtain statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to consider other factors that could impact transaction rates, such as the appeal of the donation page, the target audience, and the effectiveness of the ad campaign. By taking these factors into account and conducting well-designed experiments with higher levels of confidence, we can gather more reliable insights to inform future strategies for increasing transactions on the donation page.

experiments.title: How a callout on a gift array impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the gift array for donation page visitors will achieve more donations because it increases the value proposition. because it increases the value proposition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2024 - 06/30/2024
DTS was running their annual FYE campaign. We wanted to test how a callout that added value proposition would impact donations and encourage people to give a gift of a higher amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a callout on the gift array for donation page visitors can potentially increase donations, as evidenced by a 10.4% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.2%, which is below the threshold of 95% needed for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test different types of callouts or messaging to see which ones resonate best with donors and lead to the most significant increase in donations. It is also important to continuously iterate and experiment with different strategies to optimize donation performance.

experiments.title: How a $25 callout on the Non-Tribute donation age impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a $25 callout  for the non-tribute donation page will achieve an increase in revenue and donations because it reinforces the impact of the user’s gift in a tangible way — powering a user’s site..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2024 - 09/06/2024
For CaringBridge’s non-tribute donation page (main donate) we hypothesized that including a $25 callout over the gift array, leveraging language that’s been used successfully in other locations/widgets on the site, would improve overall revenue and transactions on main donate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a $25 callout for the non-tribute donation page resulted in a 3.0% increase in revenue for all traffic but with a statistical level of confidence of only 54%.
It is likely that this callout is not making a significant impact either way, because in both the control and treatment, the average gift is already above the $25 callout.
Moving forward, it is important to note that the results of this experiments hould not be be considered valid. It is essential to ensure that any future experiments have a higher level of statistical confidence to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the hypothesis and experiment design to potentially achieve more significant and reliable results. Testing different variations, such as adjusting the language or positioning of the callout, could also help to optimize future experiments.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including gift handles with corresponding impact descriptions in a fundraising email may not always lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease of 15.1% in donations for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid or conclusive.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies and elements in fundraising emails to see what resonates best with donors. This could include testing different gift handle amounts, impact descriptions, or even the placement of this information within the email. It's also important to ensure that experiments are conducted with a sufficient sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results.

Overall, the key takeaway is to not solely rely on the results of one experiment, especially if the statistical confidence level is below the recommended threshold. By continuing to test and iterate on different strategies, we can gather more reliable data to inform decision-making for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How including a gift array impacted clicks on a direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift array options for nondonor email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because there is greater clarity around the giving amounts and easier entry point to the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2024
During a fall recurring campaign, we wondered if introducing the gift array options for monthly donors would drive additional clicks on an appeal by introducing the decision at an earlier stage in the funnel. We split the non-donor file 50/50 and included a clickable gift array for the treatment group.
experiments.keyLearnings: The inclusion of gift array options for non-donor email recipients did not achieve the expected increase in clicks to the donation page. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 44.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. For future testing, we will consider testing different variations of the gift array options, such as the layout, wording, or placement within the email, to see if any of these factors may impact the click-through rates.

experiments.title: How adding a “Most Popular Gift” call out on the gift array impacts one-time donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a “Most Popular Gift” call out for donation page gift arrays will achieve an increase in donor conversions because it increases the clarity of what the most popular option is.. because  it increases the clarity of what the most popular option is..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2024 - 02/23/2024
Luther Seminary has seen a steady amount of traffic on their donation page. Conversions have been consistent, but the average gift has been lower. We wanted to see if we could get more conversions and also increase the number of gifts because we increase clarity of what the most popular option is for most page visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "Most Popular Gift" call out to the donation page gift arrays led to a 22.0% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical confidence level of 77.3% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and unreliable for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and account for any potential fluctuations.
Overall, the concept of highlighting a "Most Popular Gift" option shows promise in potentially increasing donor conversions and gift amounts. It could be worth conducting further experiments with adjustments to ensure reliable and conclusive results that can guide decision-making for optimizing donation pages in the future.

experiments.title: How reducing friction by removing gift amount options from a gift array affects donation rates

rows.name: 6 ask array choices for RD and 1X

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of ask arrays for potential donors will achieve greater conversion rates because we are eliminating options to decrease indecision and reduce friction from the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/03/2022
We tested this on the GAF desktop on both monthly v. one-time gifts to see if decreasing the ask arrays would eliminate indecision and increase donor conversion since we are giving fewer choices to potential donors. For RD we removed the $75 and $100. For 1X – Removed $150 and $300, added $250.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:

• One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (+12) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).

• Recurring donor conversion and RPV experienced directional declines.

• Recommendation is not to change the desktop ask array values.

• This test was the second iteration of ask array reduction tests. The first, tested mobile and yielded a strong statistically significant results.

experiments.title: How does calling out an incomplete membership with a suggested ask amount for non-members affect membership conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting in the email that their membership is incomplete and giving them a suggested gift amount  for nonmembers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it adds clarity to the ask which can cause Potential members appreciate knowing what is expected of them and how much they are expected to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for non-members to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how non-members would react to calling out in the top of the email that their membership is incomplete and providing them with a suggested gift amount to activate it. The callout also included the deadline of January 31st.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting in the email that their membership is incomplete and suggesting a gift amount for non-members can potentially increase donor conversion rate. The treatment group showed a 12.8% increase in donations compared to the control group but only with a 60.6% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment group saw a 21% decrease in clicks on the email with a 99% level of confidence.
Because the statistical level of confidence of 60.6% is below the recommended threshold of 85%, this means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to increase the sample size or conduct the experiment for a longer duration to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. This will provide more reliable results and help make informed decisions about the effectiveness of different strategies.
Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations or combinations of strategies in order to identify the most effective approach for increasing donor conversion rate. This could include testing different types of messaging, callouts, suggested gift amounts, or deadlines.
Overall, the key learning from this experiment is that highlighting membership incompleteness and providing a suggested gift amount may be a promising strategy for increasing donor conversion rate. However, further testing is needed to validate these findings and make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How preselecting the first amount on the gift array impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting the gift array amount for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because there is enhanced cognitive momentum on the page reducing friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2023 - 12/28/2023
For a high traffic donation page, we wanted to test if pre-selecting an amount for the visitor would encourage more visitors to complete the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that pre-selecting the gift array amount on the donation page may have a positive impact on the conversion rate. The treatment group, which had the pre-selected amount, saw a 12.8% increase in transactions compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 59.2%, which is below the desired threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

To further understand the impact of pre-selecting the gift array amount, it is recommended to conduct additional experiments with larger sample sizes and longer durations. This will help to increase the statistical power and confidence level of the results, providing more reliable insights.

Future experimentation should also consider other factors that could influence the conversion rate, such as the visibility and placement of the pre-selected amount, as well as the impact on different segments of visitors. By conducting rigorous experiments and analyzing the results with statistical validity, the organization can make informed decisions regarding the implementation of pre-selected amounts for donation pages.

experiments.title: How removing a premium and specific ask amount from a donation page impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the premium of a pledge coin  for visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will remove unnecessary friction from the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2023 - 01/04/2024
In an effort to increase our instant donor conversion rate for ACI, we wanted to test removing the premium of their 2023 Pledge Coin which required a $100 donation. We felt that the high ask amount and the premium was causing unnecessary friction for the user, causing them to give lower amounts or not at all.
Therefore, our hypothesis was that be removing the premium all together, potential donors would have a clearer idea of what was being asked of them due to the clarity now being provided.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the premium of the pledge coin for visitors resulted in a 11.9% increase in the number of transactions. However, due to the statistical level of confidence being below 95%, these results cannot be considered valid.
The treatment saw a negative impact in revenue at a 51.7% drop with a 16.4% level of confidence, also not considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to conduct experiments for a longer duration to collect more data and increase the statistical confidence level. While you should not conduct future decision making off of these results, we can observe how donors perceived this change. That is, more people were willing to give when there was no premium, but the average gift fell significantly. Therefore, it would be crucial that you consider these results from a strategic standpoint of focusing on donors or donations.
Overall, this experiment highlights the potential of removing unnecessary friction in the donation process to improve donor conversion. It is recommended to continue exploring different strategies to simplify the donation experience and increase revenue for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array in the body of an email appeal impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a sample gift array for a direct email appeal will achieve an increase in engagement because there will be additional cognitive momentum to continue the process to the next step.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
In a high-urgency campaign, we typically keep the decisions isolated per each step. In the email step, we help the reader decide “Do I want to click?” and then at the donation page step we help the reader decide “How much do I want to give?” In one email, we wanted to test if adding gift array options at the email level would help more people begin thinking about both questions at once. The treatment version added a gift array of buttons, letting the reader know they could click one of the buttons to choose their donation amount and begin the process of making a monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: After splitting the email between the control and the treatment, we observed a 1.2% lift in clicks at 9.4% level of confidence for the treatment version which is not statistically significant so the experiment results are not valid. The engagement remained flat, even when we added buttons with gift array amounts in the body of the email. We will want to look downstream and see if there is any significant difference in completed donations from the treatment group that would lead to more meaningful results to see if moving the gift array into the body copy helped produce more motivated visits from email traffic who had already decided what amount they would be interested in giving and continue the cognitive momentum in the process when they arrive on the donation page.

experiments.title: How lowering a gift array on an instant donation page for an acquisition offer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array for note of encouragement signers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the lower gift array aligns better with the perceived value of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
IMB is running an acquisition offer through paid Facebook ads to sign a note of encouragement for missionaries who are traveling and out in the field. They wanted to increase the donor conversion rate on the instant donation page of the acquisition offer so we decided to lower the gift array because this is a low value offer and they aren’t receiving in return like an ebook funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that lowering the gift array for note of encouragement signers did not achieve a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the lower gift array saw a significant decrease in donations of 49.3% compared to the control version.
Based on these results, it is evident that the perceived value of the offer did not align with the lower gift array. This suggests that donors may be more willing to donate higher amounts when presented with a wider range of options. Or it suggests that users see this content offer as high value and makes them want to give more to support more missionaries.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the perceived value of the offer and ensure that the gift array aligns with this value. It may be beneficial to test different gift array options to find the optimal balance between a wide range of options and the perceived value.

experiments.title: How a low-to-high gift array affects donor conversion (Why the Bible)
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that a low-to-high gift array for users on the (Why the Bible) instant donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting a more entry-level gift amount will help increase first time donors and those unable to gift a large amount but who want to support.
because  highlighting a more entry-level gift amount will help increase first time donors and those unable to gift a large amount but who want to support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 06/05/2023
Reasons to Believe orders their gift arrays from highest ask to lowest; the assumption being that starting with the larger gift will increase the average gift. However, we hypothesized that for instant donation pages on offers specifically—where perhaps a user isn’t already familiar with RTB— a $250 gift might be a daunting place to start.  We believe that lowering the barrier to entry will increase the number of gifts. This will lead to an overall increase in revenue even if the average gift is lowered due to the increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting a more entry-level gift amount on an instant donation page can potentially increase the number of first-time donors and those who cannot gift a large amount but still want to support. This hypothesis was supported by the treatment group, which saw a 64.7% increase in revenue for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, the results cannot be considered valid for future decision making. However, testing this same treatment on a page with greater sample size could help this hypothesis validate.

experiments.title: How a low-to-high gift array affected donor conversion (Noah’s Flood)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a low-to-high gift array for users on the Noah’s Flood instant donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting a more entry-level gift amount will increase first time donors and those unable to gift a large amount but who want to support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 06/05/2023
Reasons to Believe orders their gift arrays from highest ask to lowest; the assumption being that starting with the larger gift will increase the average gift. However, we hypothesized that for instant donation pages on offers specifically—where perhaps a user isn’t already familiar with RTB— a $250 gift might be a daunting place to start. We believe that lowering the barrier to entry will increase the number of gifts. This will lead to an overall increase in revenue even if the average gift is lowered due to the increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 24 days, and the results showed that the treatment group saw a 48.1% increase in revenue for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 73.4%. Though these results cannot be considered valid.
From this experiment, the key learnings are that highlighting a more entry-level gift amount increased the conversion rate for first-time donors and those who are unable to gift a large amount but want to support the cause. Furthermore, it is evident that the organization should continue testing and experimenting to find out the ideal donation page design and gift array.
In future experimentation, the organization should increase the sample size, use a longer testing period and improve the test variation to ensure higher statistical validity

experiments.title: How Lowered Gift Array on Main Donate Impacts Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array and displaying it as low to high for users on the general donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it lowers the “level of entry” for general users on the main donate page who might not be familiar with RTB..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2023 - 07/03/2023
RTB’s Main Donate page displays a high to low gift, with $500 being the first option and the suggested low amount being $50. We hypothesized that reversing this, lowering it, and suggesting an amount of $25 will increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Changing the gift array from high to low and suggesting a lower amount did not result in an increase in donor conversion. The treatment version of the experiment saw a 16.1% decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that the control version performed better in terms of conversion.
However, the statistical level of confidence for the decrease in donations for all traffic is 68.0%, which is below the 85% threshold. This was not the expected result, since this performed well on a Premium donation page and was expected to perform just well if not better on a general page. This assumption was partly rooted in that typically general donation page might have more "new users" but in reality the traffic to this page shows that RTB still receives a lot of returning visitors.
More testing is needed in order to achieve a validated learning from this experiment.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 06/29/2023
This test is a follow-up test to the “How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion” test recorded earlier this year.
We wanted to retest this concept because this topic ended up being an integral part of their Fiscal Year End campaign.
Historically, Care Net has typically run a sticky bar that dynamically populates the number of donors needed across their website during high-urgency campaigns. This year during their fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to test and see how adding a gift array to this would impact donor conversion to have the amount selected happen at this level instead of when they are redirected to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner resulted in a decrease in donor conversion. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result is below the threshold of 85%, which means these results cannot be considered valid. But because this was at the end of Care Net's fiscal year, we ended up ending the experiment early and rolling out the control to ensure 100% of the traffic saw the better-performing variant.

Based on this, it is not recommended to use the gift array in the home page sticky banner for future campaigns. Instead, it would be worthwhile to explore other strategies or elements to improve donor conversion. Future experimentation should focus on testing different approaches and measuring their impact on donor conversion to identify the most effective strategies for increasing donations.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options in the gift array affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of options on the gift array for ebook downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there are fewer decisions to make at the point of conversion, overall reducing the amount of friction involved in the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2023 - 06/09/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a study guide about the Book of Revelation. Once users download the free study guide, they are redirected to the instant donation page where they are presented with value proposition asking for a donation to Luther Seminary.
Luther Seminary wanted to increase the instant conversion rate on this page so we decided to test only having one option on the gift array versus normally having four options. We believe this will decrease the friction involved in the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of options on the gift array for ebook downloaders did not achieve a higher donor conversion rate in this particular case. The treatment group saw a decrease of 33.4% in conversion rate with a level of confidence of 61%, making the results invalid. Because this experiment did not have 50 donations minimum and did not reach a valid level of confidence, it's hard to say how this would affect other offers and organizations.

In future experimentation, we need to test this hypothesis on an offer that receives more traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: How a Monthly Callout in the Gift Array Impacts Recurring Giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the monthly giving option for users on the donation page will achieve an increase in recurring gifts because the copy reinforces the value of a monthly gift to the reader and drives home what a monthly gift accomplishes..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 05/14/2023
The Sustainers appeal is The Navigators recurring giving campaign. With users arriving on the donation page from a variety of emails, ads, and onsite assets, we hypothesized that by calling out the monthly gift option on the donation page itself would help increase recurring gifts. We believed that a simple value prop callout reminding the user of need for monthly gifts specifically would help convert those who would have originally given a one-time gift or neither at all.
experiments.keyLearnings: The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigators' Sustainers appeal showed that highlighting the monthly giving option on the donation page did not result in an increase in recurring gifts with validity. More experimentation is needed, it also prompts questions about more effective ways to test this like having the value prop callout more prominently in the copy itself or a headline.

experiments.title: How increasing the median amount of a one-time array affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the gift array amounts  for lamplighter donors will achieve an increase in overall giving without affecting recurring donors and overall donor conversions  because  the increased donation amount doesn’t affect their decision to give a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 02/23/2023
Potential donors for Prison Fellowship’s Lamplighters are presented with a tabbed donation form and given the option to give a one time gift or a recurring monthly gift.  The control donation form provides the gift arrays options of $15 (one prisoner)/$60 (4 prisoners)/$165 for an entire class.
We hypothesized that by increasing the median gift array option presented that there might be the potential to maintain the donor conversion rate and raise revenue without affecting recurring donors.   The tested gift array options for the treatment started at $30 to help 2 prisoners/$90 (6 prisoners)/$165 for an entire class.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the gift array options presented to potential donors of Prison Fellowship's Lamplighters program led to a decrease in overall number of donors; however there was essentially no effect on recurring donors in the control vs. the treatment.  As expected there was increased revenue for the treatment which presented the higher gift array including a large gift of $4,971.  When removing the large gift, the treatment received a 13% increase in revenue.
When separating out the one time and recurring donors and eliminating the large gift, the test validated for all donors and one time donors, but did not validate for recurring donors.  There was essentially no effect on recurring donors.  There were 14 recurring donors in the control and 12 in the treatment; the average gift for the recurring donors in the treatment was slightly higher - $37 vs. $33 in the control.
When focusing on one time donors we see that treatment resulted in 21% fewer donors with 20% more revenue.  Interestingly both the Control and the Treatment garnered 4 gifts of $165.  The Treatment did have 2 - $200 gifts which that plus 3 gifts in the $90 range resulted in the slight lift in revenue for the treatment's one time donors.
The higher conversion rate resulting in 25% more donors  indicates the control as the winner even considering the lower revenue that was generated.

experiments.title: How presenting a single option in a gift array for video viewers impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people will have to spent less time considering which choice to make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2022 - 12/25/2022
In a series of testing around gift array options, we wanted to test the impact on donor conversion and revenue by removing the extraneous choices from the gift array menu and offering visitors one option to select to donate $10 or type in the amount of their own choosing in the “Other” field. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and ran the test for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers increased donor conversion by 16.0%.
