However, the statistical level of confidence is only 62.8%, which means the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Future experiments should be designed with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, tests should be conducted on different target audiences to see if these results hold true for other groups. Overall, the experiment suggests that simplifying the donation process may lead to increased donations, but further testing is needed to confirm this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How a simpler gift array impacted gifts on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating decision friction for nondonors after submitting a survey will achieve an increase in $10 donations because the body copy will have synergy with the donation form elements.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2022 - 12/31/2022
When sending non-donors through a survey to an instant donation page, we wondered if removing the one time vs. monthly option and reducing the gift array from multiple options to mirror the ask in the donation page language for 3,000 supporters to donate $10 would improve conversion rate. We split traffic between the two versions and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that eliminating decision friction for non-donors after submitting a survey can result in an increase in $10 donations. The treatment saw a 15.7% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 69.3%, which is below the threshold for valid results. Therefore, the findings cannot be used for future decision making. However, the experiment provides a starting point for future experimentation to optimize the donation process, such as further testing of the language and design of the donation page. The results highlight the importance of carefully evaluating statistical significance and not relying on results below the threshold for valid experimentation.

experiments.title: How reducing the options on a gift array impacted response from non-donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array options for nondonors will achieve an increase in instant donor conversion because we are eliminating friction in the decision making process by offering one clear choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2022 - 01/15/2023
On an instant donation page during a year-end survey, we wanted to learn if eliminating friction by reducing the decision making process at the gift array level would help us get more non-donors to say “yes” when we asked for a gift. Rather than making them decide if they want to give $10, $25, $50, $100, $250, or “Other” we developed a treatment that removed all donation amounts other than “$10” and “Other” to make the ask more clear. We ran equal amounts of traffic through each experience and let the experiment run for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the gift array options for non-donors may not achieve an increase in instant donor conversion. The treatment saw a 5.9% decrease in donations for all traffic with a 73.0% statistical level of confidence, and the results cannot be considered valid.

Moreover, the experiment showed that revenue experienced a 217% decrease with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that the treatment's strategy has a negative impact on the organization's revenue, which is not ideal for a nonprofit.

While there was a 741% increase in donations from visitors aged 55-64 with a 99% level of confidence, this finding doesn't provide enough evidence that the treatment is an effective strategy. It's also unclear why this age group responded differently to the treatment.

Therefore, before making online donation changes, it's essential to consider that the gift array options available for donors may impact the organization's revenue negatively. We should test several options cautiously and analyze the data correctly. It's imperative to achieve higher statistical levels of confidence to ensure that the test's results are valid and reliable so that we can use them to make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How offering one option on the gift array impacted gifts from nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that isolating the ask in the gift array to $10 for nondonors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the ask in the copy is more aligned with the options presented in the first step of the form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2023 - 02/17/2023
In a series of testing gift array options, we wanted to better learn how the treatment was impacting conversion rate for non-donors so we continued to locate places to test this concept. One offer was an ask for 400 supporters to chip in $10 to help stop the home equity tax. Since the body copy mentioned specifically a need for $10 donations, we wanted to test if including $10 as the only option on the gift array field would help more non-donors convert to a new donor on this appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this A/B experiment conducted by the Canadian Taxpayers Federation are that isolating the ask in the gift array to $10 for non-donors did not achieve an increase in donor conversion. The treatment saw a decrease in donations for all traffic, including a 276% decrease for Mobile visitors and a 753% decrease for Direct visitors. However, due to the statistical level of confidence being below 95%, the results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact on specific visitor segments, as seen in the significant decreases in donations for Mobile and Direct visitors. It may also be beneficial to test different gift array options, including the use of specific donation amounts versus a range of amounts, to see what resonates best with donors. It is important to continue testing and analyzing results to improve conversion rates and optimize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of decisions on a gift array impacted overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of decisions on the gift array for our French speaking audience will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there will be less friction in decision making for the visitors when they make their selection.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2022 - 12/31/2022
While testing the impact of gift array choices, we wanted to see if the French-speaking audience saw similar or different results than the English-speaking audience. We created one treatment that kept the choice between one time and monthly giving yet reduced the gift array choice to just $10 or “Other” and we created a second treatment that removed the choice for monthly giving and just asked for one-time gifts of $10 using a single gift array. We split traffic 3 ways between the control and the treatment and ran the experiment for 9 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the gift array options for French-speaking audiences resulted in an increase in donations. However, we cannot confidently validate this result as the statistical level of confidence is below 95%. Moreover, the experiment showed a significant decrease in revenue, which raises concerns about the overall impact of limiting gift array options. In future experimentation, we should consider testing different gift array configurations and analyzing the impact on both donation rates and revenue to make better-informed decisions about gift array options for different audiences. It is also important to ensure that experiments are run long enough to get reliable results and to always perform a proper statistical analysis to avoid flawed conclusions.

experiments.title: How reducing the gift array choices on a video campaign impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the ask in the copy with the ask in the gift array for video viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the gift array matches up with the original ask in the body copy on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2022 - 12/25/2022
In a series of ongoing testing, we wanted to find out if the amount of choices on a gift array was helping or hindering performance. In this case, we were asking for visitors to chip in $10 if they liked the video so it could be shown to 1,000 more people. Thus, the treatment we developed isolated the gift array options to just $10 or “Other” if they wanted to give more or less than choosing the $10 option. We split traffic between the control and treatment and ran the experiment for 16 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the gift array options can potentially increase donor conversion. However, the statistically significant level of confidence of 45.8% is below the recommended level of 95%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid. Therefore, further experimentation and testing is needed to confirm the effectiveness of simplifying gift array options. Future experiments should aim to increase the sample size and statistical power to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it is important to consider other factors that could impact donor conversion, such as the messaging and design of the donation page.

experiments.title: How presenting a single option in a gift array for video viewers impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people will have to spent less time considering which choice to make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2022 - 12/25/2022
In a series of testing around gift array options, we wanted to test the impact on donor conversion and revenue by removing the extraneous choices from the gift array menu and offering visitors one option to select to donate $10 or type in the amount of their own choosing in the “Other” field. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and ran the test for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers increased donor conversion by 16.0%. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 62.8%, which means the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Future experiments should be designed with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, tests should be conducted on different target audiences to see if these results hold true for other groups. Overall, the experiment suggests that simplifying the donation process may lead to increased donations, but further testing is needed to confirm this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How eliminating gift array choices impacted instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing decision fatigue for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the donor will have to do less work to say yes to what is being asked of them on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2022 - 01/15/2023
In a series of testing around gift array presentation, we wanted to know how results would differ for a post-petition instant donation page with mostly acquisition traffic compared to a survey donation page for primarily email visitors. We set up the same treatments for this segment that we had for the others to measure the overall impact of each version. The first treatment removed the extra gift array options, keeping only $10 and other. The second treatment removed the one time vs. monthly decision from the process and kept only the $10 or “Other” options in tact. Traffic was split equally across all 3 treatments and the experiment ran for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing decision fatigue may increase donor conversion rates for instant donation page visitors, but the results cannot be considered valid due to the low statistical level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment had a negative impact on donations from visitors aged 55-64.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential negative effects of simplifying donation options, particularly for specific age groups. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of gift array options and language to determine the most effective approach for increasing donor conversion rates. It is also important to ensure that experiments have a high enough statistical level of confidence to be considered valid before implementing any changes.

experiments.title: How asking for $20 vs. $10 in a single gift array impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the amount of a single gift array choice for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in revenue because not only will more people will be saying “yes” and they will be saying “yes” to a higher amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2023 - 02/20/2023
Through previous testing, we were identifying some trends that offering donors one $10 gift array was increasing conversion in some instances so we were interested to learn if the increase in conversion was occurring because of the AMOUNT of the ask or purely the SINGULARITY of the ask. So we developed one treatment with the single array of $10 and created a second treatment that kept all elements the same except made the single gift array a $20 option instead. We split traffic equally across the 3 versions and ran the test for 5 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the amount of a single gift array choice in donation pages can result in a slight increase in donations. However, the results are not statistically significant enough to be considered valid. It's also important to note that the treatment resulted in a significant decrease in donations from male visitors and visitors aged 65+. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to test different gift array amounts and language to determine the best combination for increasing donations without negatively impacting certain visitor demographics. It's also important to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to obtain statistically significant results.

experiments.title: How a simpler gift array impacted gifts on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating decision friction for nondonors after submitting a survey will achieve an increase in $10 donations because the body copy will have synergy with the donation form elements.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2022 - 12/31/2022
When sending non-donors through a survey to an instant donation page, we wondered if removing the one time vs. monthly option and reducing the gift array from multiple options to mirror the ask in the donation page language for 3,000 supporters to donate $10 would improve conversion rate. We split traffic between the two versions and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that eliminating decision friction for non-donors after submitting a survey can result in an increase in $10 donations. The treatment saw a 15.7% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 69.3%, which is below the threshold for valid results. Therefore, the findings cannot be used for future decision making. However, the experiment provides a starting point for future experimentation to optimize the donation process, such as further testing of the language and design of the donation page. The results highlight the importance of carefully evaluating statistical significance and not relying on results below the threshold for valid experimentation.

experiments.title: How promoting a specific gift amount impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking existing donors for a specific gift amount for email audience will achieve higher conversion rate because the decision anxiety of how much to donate has been removed.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2023 - 02/08/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas that shares the truth of God’s Word through radio, television, digital resources, and books.
Each month PTV offers a different book resource to their email file that provides the audience with an opportunity to make a donation and receive the resource as an appreciation gift.
Existing donors on the PTV email file historically receive a nonspecific gift amount asked. In an effort to increase donor conversion, we decided to test a specific ask amount for this audience.
We have conducted similar testing for the nondonor email segment and have seen positive results in providing nondonors with a specific gift amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
On the email level, we did not see any significant or valid directional lifts. However, for donations and revenue, we see an interesting story...
For donations, the control saw 15 donations and 16 donations for the treatment. That calculates to a 5.2% increase with an 11.1% level of confidence.
For revenue, the control brought in $975 with an average gift of $65, and the treatment brought in $789 with an average gift of $49.31.
As a reminder, the treatment asked for a donation of $25 or more while the control was nonspecific.
The transaction break down for the two variants were
Control - nonspecific amount ask
1 - $10 gift3 - $25 gifts1 - $30 gift1 - $35 gift3 - $50 gifts1 - $75 gift3 - $100 gifts2 - $150 gifts
Treatment - $25 ask
2 - $7 gifts7 - $25 gifts4 - $50 gifts2 - $75 gifts1 - $250 gift
For revenue we saw a 13.3% decrease in overall revenue with a 61.6% level of confidence
Surprisingly, we saw that the control donors were more likely to make a gift of $25 or more without being prompted with 11 gifts. While the treatment audience was more likely to stay at the $25 level.
As this audience did not respond as well as to the specific ask as the nondonor segment has, we will begin testing into generic language around value prop and mission of PTV to see if a change will increase response.

experiments.title: How adding a middle giving option to the gift array impacted donation revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a third giving option as an “in-between” amount for monthly and one-time giving will achieve an increase in donor revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/16/2022 - 01/25/2023
The PFI Lamplighters program gives people the opportunity to support prisoners in ways that help them become closer to God. The current one-time and monthly giving options for Lamplighters are $15 to support one prisoner and $165 to support an entire class. We hypothesized that adding a middle amount as a giving option would reduce giving friction, giving donors an additional option that might nestle in nicely between an amount that is too low or not impactful enough and an amount that is too high.
To test this hypothesis, we added a $60 giving option to support four prisoners. We tested this concept already but only for monthly giving. The test resulted in a valid lift in revenue for the treatment with an average gift twice the amount of the control. The average gift for the treatment was $61.52, implying that the added $60 giving option was a common selection among donors.
Let’s see how the concept performed when testing it for monthly AND one-time giving…
experiments.keyLearnings:
The impact on revenue was minimal this time, with the treatment experiencing a 0.71% decrease in revenue but with only a 51% level of confidence. The average gift for the treatment was $39.08 and $46.95 for the control. You'll recall that when we ran the experiment only for monthly giving, the average gift was just over $60.
This tells us that adding the $60 giving option to the one-time giving gift array did not influence as much giving at that giving level as when we added it as an option only for monthly giving.
However, we saw a much greater impact in overall donations. The treatment experienced a 21% lift in total donations at an 89.3% level of confidence. Interestingly enough, the experiment specifically for monthly giving resulted in a slight decrease in donations but a significant increase in revenue.
Having three giving options "low, "middle", and "high" amounts likely reduced donation friction as potential donors did not feel the pressure of giving too little in terms of impact and too much in terms of money. They had a middle option to provide comfort in choice (a happy medium), helping to reduce anxiety. This led to more donations.
Even if a donor chose to give less than $60, we believe their desire to give period was still stronger because they didn't feel as if their gift was "small" when comparing it to the next level up, which was $60. The psychological effect may be the opposite for a gift array that had only $15 and $165 options. In this case, potential donors may choose not to give at all because a $15 gift in their minds would be too small or insignificant when compared to the other amount of $165. Ultimately, $60 provides a nice reference point for potential donors.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 43.4% increase in revenue with a 95% level of confidence – which contributed the bulk of the experiment traffic.

experiments.title: How increasing the suggested ask amount for a premium offer during year-end impacted revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the suggested ask for donors will achieve a change in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2021 - 01/04/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular offers during their end-of-year campaign is to donate a specific amount and have 4 advent candles lit for your intentions at the Shrine of Our Lady of the Snows.
Since year-end is where the majority of online revenue comes in for the Oblates, we wanted to test whether increase the ask for the Advent offer from $50 to $75 would make an impact on revenue from the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment were interesting. Overall, there was a decline in revenue per visitor of 9.5% with a 73% level of confidence. That means that, despite a higher average gift on the $75 experiment version, total revenue was less with the higher ask.

However, the results also varied by the kind of donor that landed on the page. New visitors had a decrease of 33% in revenue with a 96% level of confidence; our hypothesis was that the $75 donation was just too high for new donors.

The story for returning visitors was different, however, with a 28.6% increase in revenue and a 89% level of confidence. Those that knew the work of the Oblates, and were interested in giving again to have advent candles lit for their intentions, were willing to donate at the higher ask.

This is an experiment we will continue to refine in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a ‘most popular’ treatment to a specific gift amount impacted revenue.
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a most popular gift amount for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2022 - 04/07/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. As part of the run-up to their Easter fundraising campaign, we wanted to test whether adding a graphic treatment for the ‘most popular’ amount would have an impact on conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a slight increase in conversion rate with the most popular treatment option, as well as a slight increase in average gift. However, since it did not reach the level of confidence needed for a valid test result nor can provide any directional results, we will need to continue testing this approach to see if it has a long-term impact on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How adding a $5 minimum gift requirement impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a minimum donation amount for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2020 - 04/17/2020
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular digital acquisition offers is the Our Lady of Lourdes digital novena.
Traditionally, there was a premium associated with this offer in an effort to get ebook sign-ups to donate, with an offer of a vial of water from the sacred Lourdes grotto in France as a thank for a donor’s gift. For this experiment, we added a $5 minimum donation to the premium ask to see whether it would have an impact on donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in the $5 minimum gift, with language that made it clear the minimum was to cover shipping and handling costs, had a slight increase of 8% in terms of revenue per visitor, with a 72% level of confidence. Moreover, there was a 28% increase in overall donation conversion rates with a 98% level of confidence.

Our hypothesis is that by positioning the minimum gift requirement as a way to make sure the donor's donation is maximized (instead of spent on shipping and handling) led donors to be more generous with their gifts.

This kind of language merits continued testing throughout premium acquisition offers for the Oblates.

experiments.title: How removing a gift array from a donation page impacts revenue during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the gift array from the campaign donation page for page visitors  will achieve increased conversions and higher revenue per visitor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2021 - 07/09/2021
RTB’s donation pages always have a gift array to offer a preselected giving amount for the site visitor. For RTB’s 2021 FYE campaign, we wanted to test removing the gift array on the campaign donation page to see how this would impact giving (donations and revenue). The idea behind it is that visitors would give more without being guided by a gift array and having clear direction to choose their own giving amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While revenue only decreased 6.7% with a level of confidence of 55%, donor conversion rate had a directional decrease of 11.9% (LoC 88%). Though the data is not statistically valid, the results seem to indicate the the gift array is helpful in providing people a guide on how much they should give. If they aren't given pre-selected amounts, they are less likely to give on the donation page. It is recommended that further testing is done on the gift array. Changing the amounts, the ascending/descending order and the placement in relation to the one-time/monthly option are all potential next steps for testing.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 101.6% increase in revenue with a 94% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 139.4% increase in revenue with an 89% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 131.7% increase in revenue with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing a gift array on a donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the gift array  for donation page visitors will achieve more donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2021 - 07/29/2021
RTB had previously tested removing the gift array from a donation page during a high urgency campaign but the results were inconclusive. In order to try and garner valid results, they retested this concept again at another point of their high urgency campaign, on a different donation page. The hypothesis is that removing the gift array from the donation page may increase revenue and/or donor conversion rate as prospective donors feel like they have more autonomy in the giving process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Removing the gift array resulted in fewer conversions and a decrease in revenue. The experiment results indicate that donation page visitors give more and give greater when they have a gift array available for guidance. A gift array takes some of the decision out of play for potential donors; instead of a donor having to think of a giving amount on their own, they instead have options presented to them, easing their decision-making.
Furthermore, removing the gift array resulted in lower revenue. This shows that a donor's "best gift" is often lower than what it would be if

experiments.title: How reducing gift array amounts affect donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array amounts for main donate page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2021 - 01/27/2022
RTB’s main donate page begins its gift array at $500 which is significantly higher than other organization’s gift arrays. We hypothesize that by reducing the gift array amounts (starting the array at $250 instead), we’ll see a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though this experiment ran for a significant amount of time in order to get a larger sample size, this experiment did not validate. We did not see a directional lift in either donor conversion rate or revenue.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 19.4% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 28.2% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 63.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a gift array with extended description affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a gift array with extended description affect conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/07/2020
Compassion International was running a campaign to ask existing sponsors to make an additional gift to help their fellow sponsors keep their children during the economic hardship caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. They had already learned that the value proposition of “helping a sponsor” versus “helping a child” was more impactful to conversion. However, they wondered if the suggested amount of $114 (which covers three months of sponsorship) was too nebulous and random for the visitor. They created a gift array that still suggested $114, but added a line of copy that explained that “$114 covers three months of sponsorship for a fellow sponsor”. Then, the array offered $74 (two months) and $38 (one month). They hypothesized that the array would help the prospective donor understand the reasoning behind the gift amount meant and make them more likely to convert. They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment, with the gift array and additional descriptions, produced a 7.3% decrease with no statistical level of confidence. This ultimately means that the array was ineffective at moving conversion at all.

experiments.title: How using a smaller gift array impacted donor conversion on acquisition forms
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a smaller gift array increase donor conversion rate on acquisition forms?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2020 - 02/28/2020
In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate, Americans for Prosperity experimented with whether or not they were asking too much of 1st time donors. So, they wanted to test a smaller gift array to see if it would boost donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment didn’t validate (achieving only a 56% level of confidence), we noticed an 18% decrease in donor conversion with this experience.
We also noticed that revenue decreased proportionately to the decrease in the donor conversion rate, showing a 19% decrease, which was largely driven by less volume in the treatment experience, because the average gift was only about 2% lower for the treatment.
From here, we recommend further experimentation with variable gift arrays, but note that it may not have that much of a difference in terms of acquiring new donors.

experiments.title: How a “most popular” pre-selected value in the gift array did not hurt conversion or revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a visual treatment using that increases the visibility of the pre-selected value increase the average gift from instant donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2019 - 11/04/2019
In an attempt to increase the average gift for The Fund for American Studies’ acquisition campaign, we decided to experiment with a “most popular” treatment on the $50 amount in the gift array to see if it would increase the average gift amount from instant donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we did not see a validated increase in either donor conversion rate or revenue, we observed a directional lift in both metrics.
The key learning with this experiment was that after running for a month, this treatment DID NOT show, at any point, a decrease in either one of these metrics, which was enough for us to consider that the lifts we achieved were directional, and that there would not be risk in rolling this treatment out as the “new control” version of the donation page.

experiments.title: How communicating value with the gift array affects revenue and member acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation form will produce the most revenue and new members for the organization?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/29/2015 - 08/27/2015
The Texas State Historical Association is a membership-based organization with varying levels of benefits at each member level. The idea is to incentivize members to increase their giving in order to raise the average gift.
The most common donation form listed out each membership level and the associated benefits. This design led to a long donation form that was heavy on the copy. Our concern was that this structure produced additional friction to the giving process which would, in turn, lower conversion.  We wanted to find a way to decrease the size of the form while still communicating the value of each membership level. We decided to test a new form that utilized stylized buttons and a single, dynamic paragraph to describe the associated benefits of their selected level.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new treatment with stylized buttons produced no significant lift to either average gift or conversion. We were able to reduce friction on the form but, by hiding the benefits of each membership level, we equally reduced the incentive. These two factors offset each other which resulted a no significant change.

experiments.title: How a higher gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a gift array with higher values affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2019 - 03/14/2019
CaringBridge had used a giving infrastructure that had separate mobile and desktop donation pages. As a result, certain optimizations had not been rolled out to both experiences, resulting in slight differences between the two pages. In previous tests, CaringBridge had “unified” the copy on the page by testing out of all negative impact.
However, the mobile version still had another gift array that started at $25, unlike the others that started at $50. As their final “unification” test, CaringBridge wanted to test the $50 array that was found everywhere else to make sure there was no impact on revenue.
The control had an array of $25, $50, $100, $250. They launched a treatment with the array found on the other pages: $50, $100, $250, Other.
experiments.keyLearnings: After more than 700 transactions, the new gift array had no significant difference in conversions, which gave CaringBridge the all-clear to launch the unified experience. This process, though it took time, is the proof-positive way to launch new experiences. CaringBridge could have easily just launched the same copy and donation array everywhere, but there could have been serious ramifications in revenue. This series of unification tests shows that there was no risk at all.

experiments.title: How the gift array options impacted Leaders Club memberships
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a lower gift array of $3, $10, & $15 increase the conversion rate and overall revenue to the Leaders Club program?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2016 - 11/10/2016
For a recent Leaders Club campaign, we wanted to test the standard gift array of $5, $15, & $25 to see if we could lower the mental friction associated with the high monthly ask. Our hypothesis was that if we can reduce the initial ask amount, we could get more donors which would lift the overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: The lower gift array did not have any statistically significant impact on a donor’s likelihood to join the Leaders Club or their gift amount once they joined. It is recommended that we maintain the current control of $5, $10, and $15 for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How the gift array impact a recurring giving campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How does a blank gift array impact average gift and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/05/2017 - 07/19/2017
Unfortunately, with this experiment, we did not see enough conversions to draw any meaningful analysis from the results. However, this does seem like an interesting test given the low average gift for recurring donors. It would be worth repeating with a future campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: For the Liberty Brigade acquisition campaign, we wanted to see what kind of impact the giving array had on the likelihood to join and the average gift. We had the hypothesis that using a blank array would allow people to “choose their own generosity” which would result in a higher gift.

experiments.title: How removing a gift array on mobile devices affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does an open field on a donation form affect donor conversion rate on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2017 - 06/15/2017
CaringBridge had seen a significant lift in donor conversion on one of their donation pages when they removed the gift array and simply offered an “open field” that allowed donors to pick their own donation amount. However, their donation page setup split out desktop pages and mobile pages, so they wanted to test the exact same premise on their mobile pages.
They removed the array on a treatment page and left an open field, then launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though the experiment didn’t validate, CaringBridge saw quite the opposite result on mobile devices—a 10.5% decrease in conversion when the open field was presented. This could be due to the fact that the buttons become larger on mobile devices and are much easier to use on a small screen. It could also be related to the situational difference between desktop (likely seated at a desk) and mobile (out and about, with many more possible distractions).

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a year end donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does adding gift array buttons to a donation form affect donor conversion and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 01/03/2018
Buckner International was running their year-end campaign and wanted to test how adding a gift array to their year-end donation page would affect donor conversion and gift value. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a 10% decrease to conversion rate (not statistically significant) but a 42% increase to revenue per visitor when we removed the buttons during year end (not statistically significant). Given the low significance, this is something that would need to be re-tested in future appeals before making any definitive statements. However, based upon these results, it may make sense to segment high dollar donors ($100+) and provide them with a different giving experience than lower level and non-donors. Basically, use the buttons to remove friction for the less motivated audience and then allow the higher donors to choose their own level of generosity. Again, this is a hypothesis that will need to be revalidated with a future test.
